Also, you can choose to export user permissions, which is helpful for user audit purposes.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">The <strong>Resource access</strong> report shows a timeline of selected users who have accessed various resources in a given time frame.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">The <strong>Resource modifications</strong> report is a list of users who have modified resources in a given time frame with all essential information, such as the commit message.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">The <strong>Failed login attempts</strong> report is self-explanatory; it shows all failed logins. This is an essential report for IT administrators who audit server security.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">Finally, the <strong>Audit report</strong> is a list of users’ actions related to resources. Exported data shows which users were granted or lost access to a resource, the time of the action, and who performed it.</span></li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Reports_application.png" /></ac:image></span>Reports application.</h6>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137991566 space=MCS2024x version=4 -->
## PAGE 00023: Backup and restore data procedures

- page_id: `137991566`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137991566/Backup+and+restore+data+procedures
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-12-11T14:43:30.743+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Administration Guide > Magic Collaboration Studio administration
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

**5**

**Scripts**

The following are the script files used in this installation:

- [ATTACHMENT filename='backup.sh']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='backup_all.sh']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='restore-single_node_311.sh']

This page provides the instructions for cold and hot backups, and the scripts required to perform hot backup and restore of the Cassandra database. These backup and restore scripts should be executed on a Linux server where Cassandra 4 is running.

##### Cold Backup/Restore

A cold backup is a backup taken via regular system backups, while the database is shut down in a consistent state. Once the database is shut down in a consistent state, we back up the directories pointed to by the **data_file_directories** and **commitlog_directory** values in your **cassandra.yaml** file.

Backup procedure consists of:

- Stop the WebApp service
- Stop the Teamwork Cloud service
- Stop the Authserver service
- Commit all the data to disk by issuing the "nodetool drain" command
- Stop the Cassandra service
- Perform the backup (this can be a VM snapshot, a backup of the specified directories using any backup software, or copying the directories to another location)
- Start the Cassandra service
- Start the Teamwork Cloud service
- Start the Authserver service
- Start the WebApp service

Restore procedure consists of:

- Stop the WebApp service
- Stop the Teamwork Cloud service
- Stop the Authserver service
- Stop the Cassandra service
- Delete the data_file_directories and commitlog_directory
- Restore the data from the backup to the original location
- Start the Cassandra service
- Start the Teamwork Cloud service
- Start the Authserver service
- Start WebApp service

##### Hot Backups

###### Backups

Hot backups are backups which are taken while the database is running, therefore eliminating the need for downtime. This is accomplished by using Cassandra's internal ability to perform a snapshot of the database. The backup process consists of flushing data to disk, stopping any compactions which may be taking place, creating a snapshot (the snapshot creates a set of hard links to the current immutable files), archiving the state of the immutable files to a backup location, and clearing the snapshots directories created in the database. For disaster recovery, this directory should be located on a different physical drive than where the data resides. Each snapshot will consume approximately the same amount of space as the data directory. The backup script does not perform any directory maintenance or purging. This can be done either by using a wrapper script which will delete the current backup before creating the new snapshot, or by a scheduled task which is to be executed before the backup. If point in time recovery is desired, the backup directory can be backed up as part of the regular backup procedures for the server.

When backing up a cluster, all nodes must be backed up individually, preferably at the same time. **Also, on a cluster, it is imperative that the clocks on all machines be synchronized.**

Hot backups should be executed at a time when there is no client activity, in order to ensure model consistency. If you cannot be sure that there is no client traffic at the time of the snapshot, please stop the Teamwork Cloud service momentarily. Once the “nodetool snapshot” command has completed, you can restart the Magic Collaboration Studio service.

###### Restore

The restore procedure will stop the Cassandra database, delete the commit logs and the data files in the repository, restore the snapshot files and relocate them back to their source directories. Once this is done, Cassandra will be started and the nodetool utility will be invoked to repair the keyspaces.

When restoring a cluster, all nodes must be restored from their individual backups (backups taken at the same time). You must first stop the Magic Collaboration Studio, authserver, and Cassandra service on each of the nodes. Once the Cassandra nodes are off, proceed to restore the seed node. Once you have completed the restoration of the seed node, proceed to restore the second node. Upon completion of the second node, restore the third node. During the restoration process of the first 2 nodes, you may receive an error from the keyspace repair process - this is normal since some of the nodes have not joined the cluster. The last node to be restored will take care of the keyspace repairing process.

##### Backup and restore scripts

scripts

To backup and restore Cassandra database, you need the following files. Click the file to download it.

[ATTACHMENT filename='backup.sh']

[ATTACHMENT filename='restore-single_node_311.sh']

###### backup.sh

This script creates snapshots of Cassandra database. The script must run on the host running Cassandra and Cassandra must be running. The Cassandra bin directory or nodetool must be added to **$PATH** (on Linux, nodetool is available when the Cassandra package is installed without further modifications). The backup script preserves the database file permissions and owner. On Linux, you should be running on a shell with root permissions.

The command format for backing up is:

```bash

```

where **CASSANDRA_DB_PATH** is the directory below where the Cassandra data is located, and **BACKUP_DIR** is the location in which the snapshot archives are to be saved.

The archive is named using a pattern **cassandra_backup_yyyy.mm.dd-hh.mm.ss.tar**

If any of the parameters are omitted then the script will prompt you for the locations, and perform sanity checks to make sure the values are correct.

#### NOTE: Path names

Path names

When calling the backup/restore scripts passing parameters, if your path names contain spaces, they must be quoted.

For example:

./backup.sh -dir "/path to data containing spaces/data" -rf "/path to backups containing spaces/"

#### NOTE: Windows snapshots

Windows snapshots

Due to the manner in which hard links (used by the snapshot process) are handled by Windows, existing snapshots do not get cleared until the Cassandra service is restarted. Therefore, on Windows, it is recommended to schedule a task to be performed during maintenance to restart Cassandra to complete the clearing process. After restarting Cassandra, the Magic Collaboration Studio and Authserver services should also be restarted.

###### restore-single_node_311.sh

This script should be run as root on Linux and Cygwin console must be launched with the administrator privileges. This script restores data from the backup file. While restoring Cassandra, Magic Collaboration Studio server must be turned off. The script stops and starts Cassandra if needed. The backup script preserves the database file permissions and owner, and then restores backup on another machine with another user. Therefore, the file permissions must be changed manually otherwise Cassandra may not start. The following variables may be passed onto the script:

```text
] [--commitlog ] [--rf ] [--cassandra ] [-h|--help]
--dir: cassandra database path (default:cassandra_database)
--commitlog: commitlog path (default: commitlog_path)
--rf: full path to snapshot archive (default: rf)
--cassandra: cassandra installation path when cassandra is not executed as a service (default: cassandra)
-h,--help: Prints help]]>
```

Even though parameters can be passed to the restore script, restores are typically carried out in an interactive manner.

| Variable | Description |
| --- | --- |
| commitlog_path | The path of commitlog files. They are removed when restoring database to prevent recovery of commits made earlier. DataStax recommends to store the commitlog files in a separate hard-disk for performance sake. |
| cassandra_database | The Cassandra database path. It is used to clean existing database and restoring database from the recovery file. |
| cassandra | The Cassandra installation path. It is the required parameter when Cassandra is launched not as service. |
| service | The Boolean parameter indicating that Cassandra is started as a service. The default value is true so you need to set this variable to false when Cassandra is launched by the other script or manually. |
| rf | The recovery file with the full path to it. |

###### **On Linux**

Type:

```bash

```

or

```bash

```

If you are logged in as root, the command is:

```bash

```

###### **On Windows**

Use the Cygwin tool and type:

```bash

```

The following are some other examples for Linux (just remove 'su' parameter on Cygwin):

```js
commitlog_path= rf= su ./restore-single_node.sh


Example
$ cassandra_database=/var/lib/cassandra commitlog_path=/var/lib/cassandra/commitlog rf=/home//backups/cassandra_backup_2016.07.29.12.23.24.tar su ./restore-single_node.sh

Parameters passed on the command line


$ su  ./restore-single_node.sh -dir  -commitlog  -rf 
Example
$ su  ./restore-single_node.sh -dir /var/lib/cassandra -commitlog /var/lib/cassandra/commitlog -rf /home//backups/cassandra_backup_2016.07.29.12.23.24.tar]]>
```

Cassandra should be running as a service, but if for some reason you are running it as an application then you must set the variable service to *false*. See the following example:

```text

```

You will be then asked to enter the following information:

```js
/backups/:
or parameters can be set(please note that order maters). i.e. :
$ su service=false ./restore-single_node.sh -dir  -commitlog  -rf  -cassandra=
$ su service=false ./restore-single_node.sh -dir /var/lib/cassandra -commitlog /var/lib/cassandra/commitlog -rf /home//backups/cassandra_backup_2016.07.29.12.23.24.tar -cassandra=/opt/apache-cassandra-2.2.5]]>
```

To learn how to delete resources, restore projects, and other administrative processes, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Data Manager' space='TWCloud2024x'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4d7c24ff-c5b8-432e-8440-2fa6fbef49ef"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">5</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Scripts</strong></p><p>The following are the script files used in this installation:</p><ul><li data-uuid="1ba0de3b-f550-4916-8566-5b796f6cd915"><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="backup.sh" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="98d8b0f4-77a8-4f25-be47-91172048b58c"><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="backup_all.sh" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="e2beaa4c-4b01-4e34-8144-5fd2574ce766"><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="restore-single_node_311.sh" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target">This page provides the instructions for cold and hot backups, and the scripts required to perform hot backup and restore of the Cassandra database. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">These backup and restore scripts should be executed on a Linux server where Cassandra 4 is running</span>.</p><h3>Cold Backup/Restore</h3><p>A cold backup is a backup taken via regular system backups, while the database is shut down in a consistent state. Once the database is shut down in a consistent state, we back up the directories pointed to by the <strong>data_file_directories</strong> and <strong>commitlog_directory</strong> values in your <strong>cassandra.yaml</strong> file.</p><p>Backup procedure consists of:</p><ul><li data-uuid="764e5fbb-e6de-4a8d-a01a-f8c68122a16d">Stop the WebApp service</li><li data-uuid="1b92c4d6-5835-4e64-af23-4bc4a758c5ca">Stop the Teamwork Cloud service</li><li data-uuid="6bffd7dc-6fca-40f6-9625-1a8e831764a4">Stop the Authserver service</li><li data-uuid="5ca0dd79-3aac-4bd1-98ac-7e1e42422dda">Commit all the data to disk by issuing the &quot;nodetool drain&quot; command</li><li data-uuid="56a13b6d-ae58-44e7-ba9e-52e627269722">Stop the Cassandra service</li><li data-uuid="6e06b805-f24e-4d09-8d38-4c31e51f7919">Perform the backup (this can be a VM snapshot, a backup of the specified directories using any backup software, or copying the directories to another location)</li><li data-uuid="8b59249f-37a8-4f51-bf3b-dc9a479cf3d2">Start the Cassandra service</li><li data-uuid="ae028195-7642-417a-a208-3e8f7182f8eb">Start the Teamwork Cloud service</li><li data-uuid="8bf9cce4-6382-45e4-8095-27cdbe877c56">Start the Authserver service</li><li data-uuid="cd07950e-489b-4036-a8c8-3b27de7e5999">Start the WebApp service</li></ul><p>Restore procedure consists of:</p><ul><li data-uuid="2fce8bea-e680-47cd-84c5-d285bc86571a">Stop the WebApp service</li><li data-uuid="daedea98-7cb5-4314-8d3e-2f555d722455">Stop the Teamwork Cloud service</li><li data-uuid="1a9a64f4-cdbc-4637-8114-213b5f6afb3e">Stop the Authserver service</li><li data-uuid="9a96c36d-95c0-4757-9926-46c8682757ce">Stop the Cassandra service</li><li data-uuid="5a003fa3-e7da-496b-8c39-7976838144b9">Delete the <strong>data_file_directories</strong> and <strong>commitlog_directory</strong></li><li data-uuid="9713f36a-e803-4399-be56-65c0d61cfbbb">Restore the data from the backup to the original location</li><li data-uuid="84de1ed5-57a8-48c8-97f8-6b7862a44b9a">Start the Cassandra service</li><li data-uuid="3051dfc4-356b-4556-a7e4-89baa3afb8eb">Start the Teamwork Cloud service</li><li data-uuid="98d85387-6ff5-44aa-9b82-7b15e65aea38">Start the Authserver service</li><li data-uuid="572a7998-ebc3-4055-b8ce-8e319bf6f2b9">Start WebApp service</li></ul><h3>Hot Backups</h3><h4>Backups</h4><p>Hot backups are backups which are taken while the database is running, therefore eliminating the need for downtime. This is accomplished by using Cassandra's internal ability to perform a snapshot of the database. The backup process consists of flushing data to disk, stopping any compactions which may be taking place, creating a snapshot (the snapshot creates a set of hard links to the current immutable files), archiving the state of the immutable files to a backup location, and clearing the snapshots directories created in the database. For disaster recovery, this directory should be located on a different physical drive than where the data resides.  Each snapshot will consume approximately the same amount of space as the data directory. The backup script does not perform any directory maintenance or purging. This can be done either by using a wrapper script which will delete the current backup before creating the new snapshot, or by a scheduled task which is to be executed before the backup. If point in time recovery is desired, the backup directory can be backed up as part of the regular backup procedures for the server.</p><p>When backing up a cluster, all nodes must be backed up individually, preferably at the same time. <strong>Also, on a cluster, it is imperative that the clocks on all machines be synchronized.</strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5b410d9f-9414-467d-b3b2-fd62a0e88135"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Hot backups should be executed at a time when there is no client activity, in order to ensure model consistency. If you cannot be sure that there is no client traffic at the time of the snapshot, please stop the Teamwork Cloud service momentarily. Once the “nodetool snapshot” command has completed, you can restart the Magic Collaboration Studio service.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Restore</h4><p>The restore procedure will stop the Cassandra database, delete the commit logs and the data files in the repository, restore the snapshot files and relocate them back to their source directories. Once this is done, Cassandra will be started and the nodetool utility will be invoked to repair the keyspaces. </p><p>When restoring a cluster, all nodes must be restored from their individual backups (backups taken at the same time). You must first stop the Magic Collaboration Studio, authserver, and Cassandra service on each of the nodes. Once the Cassandra nodes are off, proceed to restore the seed node. Once you have completed the restoration of the seed node, proceed to restore the second node. Upon completion of the second node, restore the third node. During the restoration process of the first 2 nodes, you may receive an error from the keyspace repair process - this is normal since some of the nodes have not joined the cluster. The last node to be restored will take care of the keyspace repairing process.</p><h3>Backup and restore scripts</h3><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="37dc3ba8-c974-4c91-9997-85ad55cbd582"><ac:parameter ac:name="">scripts</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p>To backup and restore Cassandra database, you need the following files. Click the file to download it.</p><p><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="backup.sh" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="restore-single_node_311.sh" /></ac:link> </p><h4>backup.sh</h4><p>This script creates snapshots of Cassandra database. The script must run on the host running Cassandra and Cassandra must be running. The Cassandra bin directory or nodetool must be added to <strong>$PATH</strong> (on Linux, nodetool is available when the Cassandra package is installed without further modifications). The backup script preserves the database file permissions and owner. On Linux, you should be running on a shell with root permissions.</p><p>The command format for backing up is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f8ba3a96-d1f9-4f14-bffe-79fb1fe998c7"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[./backup.sh -dir CASSANDRA_DB_PATH -rf BACKUP_DIR]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>where <strong>CASSANDRA_DB_PATH</strong> is the directory below where the Cassandra data is located, and <strong>BACKUP_DIR</strong> is the location in which the snapshot archives are to be saved.</p><p>The archive is named using a pattern <strong>cassandra_backup_yyyy.mm.dd-hh.mm.ss.tar</strong></p><p>If any of the parameters are omitted then the script will prompt you for the locations, and perform sanity checks to make sure the values are correct.</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0bdf87f2-5085-4c99-b153-be5833f7b621"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Path names</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When calling the backup/restore scripts passing parameters, if your path names contain spaces, they must be quoted.</p><p>For example:</p><p>./backup.sh -dir &quot;/path to data containing spaces/data&quot; -rf &quot;/path to backups containing spaces/&quot;</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b68d75e8-17a1-44ed-ad71-d51bafb84f43"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Windows snapshots</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Due to the manner in which hard links (used by the snapshot process) are handled by Windows, existing snapshots do not get cleared until the Cassandra service is restarted. Therefore, on Windows, it is recommended to schedule a task to be performed during maintenance to restart Cassandra to complete the clearing process. After restarting Cassandra, the Magic Collaboration Studio and Authserver services should also be restarted.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>restore-single_node_311.sh</h4><p>This script should be run as root on Linux and Cygwin console must be launched with the administrator privileges. This script restores data from the backup file. While restoring Cassandra, Magic Collaboration Studio server must be turned off. The script stops and starts Cassandra if needed. The backup script preserves the database file permissions and owner, and then restores backup on another machine with another user. Therefore, the file permissions must be changed manually otherwise Cassandra may not start. The following variables may be passed onto the script:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bf8b5e6c-4f61-4ac5-aeab-f58d1034e516"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Usage: ./restore-single_node_311.sh [--dir <arg>] [--commitlog <arg>] [--rf <arg>] [--cassandra <arg>] [-h|--help]
--dir: cassandra database path (default:cassandra_database)
--commitlog: commitlog path (default: commitlog_path)
--rf: full path to snapshot archive (default: rf)
--cassandra: cassandra installation path when cassandra is not executed as a service (default: cassandra)
-h,--help: Prints help]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Even though parameters can be passed to the restore script, restores are typically carried out in an interactive manner.</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Variable</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td><strong>commitlog_path</strong></td><td>The path of commitlog files. They are removed when restoring database to prevent recovery of commits made earlier. DataStax recommends to store the commitlog files in a separate hard-disk for performance sake.</td></tr><tr><td><strong>cassandra_database</strong></td><td>The Cassandra database path. It is used to clean existing database and restoring database from the recovery file.</td></tr><tr><td><strong>cassandra</strong></td><td>The Cassandra installation path. It is the required parameter when Cassandra is launched not as service.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>service</strong></td><td colspan="1">The Boolean parameter indicating that Cassandra is started as a service. The default value is true so you need to set this variable to <em>false</em> when Cassandra is launched by the other script or manually.</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><strong>rf</strong></td><td colspan="1">The recovery file with the full path to it.</td></tr></tbody></table><h4><strong>On Linux</strong></h4><p>Type: </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9227c5ec-45ce-40da-9d33-37928be9e017"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$ su ./restore-single_node_311.sh ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">or</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06f669d0-dce6-4748-9ac2-7902a51e4326"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$ sudo su
$ ./restore-single_node_311.sh]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If you are logged in as root, the command is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9988a4a2-2a97-4cb7-8457-5ae3ef3970ef"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$ ./restore-single_node_311.sh]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4><strong>On Windows</strong></h4><p>Use the Cygwin tool and type: </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="eec6bed8-cb12-4aa9-90f7-b3247736dbe1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$ ./restore-single_node_311.sh]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>The following are some other examples for Linux (just remove 'su' parameter on Cygwin):</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c4641b4d-c6dc-427f-8cab-b24d542de307"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">js</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Parameters passed as environment variables


$ cassandra_database=<cassandra_database_path> commitlog_path=<cassandra_commitlog_path> rf=<backup_file_to_restore> su ./restore-single_node.sh


Example
$ cassandra_database=/var/lib/cassandra commitlog_path=/var/lib/cassandra/commitlog rf=/home/<user>/backups/cassandra_backup_2016.07.29.12.23.24.tar su ./restore-single_node.sh

Parameters passed on the command line


$ su  ./restore-single_node.sh -dir <cassandra_database_path> -commitlog <cassandra_commitlog_path> -rf <backup_file_to_restore>
Example
$ su  ./restore-single_node.sh -dir /var/lib/cassandra -commitlog /var/lib/cassandra/commitlog -rf /home/<user>/backups/cassandra_backup_2016.07.29.12.23.24.tar]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Cassandra should be running as a service, but if for some reason you are running it as an application then you must set the variable service to <em>false</em>. See the following example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0fb1c18-9b4c-4d8f-876a-b98fd3f25e4a"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$ service=false su ./restore-single_node.sh]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>You will be then asked to enter the following information:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="feeef321-f73d-4fdf-929c-80d6241134f0"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">js</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Please enter Cassandra database directory. i.e. /var/lib/cassandra:
Please enter Cassandra commitlog directory. i.e. /var/lib/cassandra/commitlog:
Please enter Cassandra home directory. i.e. /opt/cassandra-2.2.5:
Please enter backup file location. i.e. /home/<user>/backups/:
or parameters can be set(please note that order maters). i.e. :
$ su service=false ./restore-single_node.sh -dir <cassandra_database_path> -commitlog <cassandra_commitlog_path> -rf <backup_file_to_restore> -cassandra=<cassandra_home>
$ su service=false ./restore-single_node.sh -dir /var/lib/cassandra -commitlog /var/lib/cassandra/commitlog -rf /home/<user>/backups/cassandra_backup_2016.07.29.12.23.24.tar -cassandra=/opt/apache-cassandra-2.2.5]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br />To learn how to delete resources, restore projects, and other administrative processes, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="TWCloud2024x" ri:content-title="Data Manager" /></ac:link>.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=275415362 space=MCS2024x version=4 -->
## PAGE 00024: Building Magic Collaboration Studio containers

- page_id: `275415362`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/275415362/Building+Magic+Collaboration+Studio+containers
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2026-03-18T10:20:45.832+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Deployment Guide > Enterprise deployment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

do

not provide Docker support

https://docs.docker.com/

**On this page**

5

**Example package**

**[ATTACHMENT filename='TWC-Container-Example-Pkg.zip']**

This page provides an example package for building Cassandra and Magic Collaboration Studio components from a Magic Collaboration Studio No-Install package. The example package uses Docker Compose to build all the components required to deploy a fully-functional Magic Collaboration Studio containerized environment. Such an environment can be deployed in an orchestration framework to enhance scaling and management.

##### **Magic Collaboration Studio Container Example Package Components**

Docker Hub Pulled Images:

- Cassandra 4.1.1
- Zookeeper 3.7.0
- ZooNavigator 1.1.1 (elkozmon) (optional)
- Elasticsearch 7.16.2

External Packages:

- Magic Collaboration Studio 2024x No-Install Linux (downloaded from the 3DS software download page )
- ActiveMQ Artemis 2.26.0 (downloaded automatically after executing the containerization environment staging script)

##### Example with Docker Compose

The example package requires at least 32GB of available RAM.

1. Download and extract the [ATTACHMENT filename='TWC-Container-Example-Pkg.zip'] into an empty working directory.
2. Download a 2024x or later version of Magic Collaboration Studio No-Install Linux package.
3. Rename or symbolically link the no-install package to twcoudsuite.zip .
4. Execute the [config.tw](http://config.tw)-compose.sh script to stage the containerization environment.
5. Run the following command to initialize Docker Compose container configurations: bashDJangotrue
6. Run the following command to launch containers: bashDJangotrue If you execute this command for the first time, it will build the containers as well.
7. Use the following URLs:
  - To access web UI: bash
  - To connect from a modeling tool client (with default port 3579): bash
  - To access REST API/Swagger Page: bash

You can apply the Magic Collaboration Studio license [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying Magic Collaboration Studio license' space=''] or [REST API](https://osmc.nomagic.com/).

##### Helpful commands

To follow the output (tail) the output of a specific container:

```bash
]]>
```

To stop all containers but retain data in non-persistent storage:

```bash

```

To stop all and remove all running containers (non-persistent storage data will be lost):

```bash

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="69c084ff-6edb-4731-b939-1844baa7849e"><ac:rich-text-body>These procedures are intended for users with Docker knowledge. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">We <strong>do </strong></span><strong>not provide Docker support</strong>, just provide guidelines on how to use it with our product. For more information about Docker, see <a href="https://docs.docker.com/">https://docs.docker.com/</a></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0c90400c-4d84-4d5e-9ca4-04dc442121ce"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">5</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Example package</strong></p><p><strong><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="TWC-Container-Example-Pkg.zip" /></ac:link></strong></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">This page provides an example package for building Cassandra and Magic Collaboration Studio components from a Magic Collaboration Studio No-Install package. The example package uses Docker Compose to build all the components required to deploy a fully-functional Magic Collaboration Studio containerized environment. Such an environment can be deployed in an orchestration framework to enhance scaling and management.</span></p><h3 style="text-align: left;"><strong>Magic Collaboration Studio Container Example Package Components</strong></h3><p style="text-align: left;">Docker Hub Pulled Images:</p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li data-uuid="2a1234fb-7256-48bc-896a-90b29f989cdc">Cassandra 4.1.1</li><li data-uuid="b2a2b4b8-ee18-4009-a3c1-8da69dd633a8">Zookeeper 3.7.0</li><li data-uuid="c02dcf5e-78d9-4cb1-bdba-9186c0d768b9">ZooNavigator 1.1.1 (elkozmon) (optional)</li><li data-uuid="cc11db59-cea7-40fa-9418-0a5b725fa4b8">Elasticsearch 7.16.2 </li></ul><p style="text-align: left;">External Packages:</p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li data-uuid="1c5e1634-6a94-412a-a91f-df9fea9a2efa">Magic Collaboration Studio 2024x No-Install Linux (downloaded from the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">3DS software download page</span>)</li><li data-uuid="17359d8c-9251-44b0-b729-fd8dca2f1753">ActiveMQ Artemis 2.26.0 (downloaded automatically after executing the containerization environment staging script)</li></ul><h3>Example with Docker Compose</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="38ac89a2-0884-4dd7-867f-57119d5a5f80"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The example package requires at least 32GB of available RAM.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ol><li data-uuid="ab3ddb65-f00a-4442-828e-faa593401c28">Download and extract the <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="TWC-Container-Example-Pkg.zip" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[example package]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> into an empty working directory.</li><li data-uuid="c67097f6-71de-4afb-9ae0-65fe2dd66bea">Download a 2024x or later version of Magic Collaboration Studio No-Install Linux package.</li><li data-uuid="926c8fc5-c7df-486f-ad00-ddbf88423d2c">Rename or symbolically link the no-install package to <em>twcoudsuite.zip</em>.</li><li data-uuid="d4b54795-c412-4c56-86bd-d172a5e75557">Execute the <em><a href="http://config.tw">config.tw</a>-compose.sh</em> script to stage the containerization environment.</li><li data-uuid="d5beb236-00ed-477e-b1ec-b3349c0f8276"><p>Run the following command to initialize Docker Compose container configurations:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="88a84211-687f-4e90-ae73-bd61ca3d5923"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker compose build]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="aef7c848-1b3d-40de-bea5-78997bb51b21"><p>Run the following command to launch containers:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a97ed20c-b2b0-456f-a2e3-10515e424664"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker compose up -d]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If you execute this command for the first time, it will build the containers as well.</p></li><li data-uuid="af73acda-66c3-4d17-a155-74ed7645de22">Use the following URLs:<ul><li><p>To access web UI:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="29a06200-acdf-47f2-9f3c-136c20893aed"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[https://host.docker.internal:8443/webapp]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p>To connect from a modeling tool client (with default port 3579):</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dc9c6862-7dc4-40af-9767-cf7afac6f62d"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[host.docker.internal]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"> To access REST API/Swagger Page:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="34ecaacf-05b4-4a1b-a6bd-9197bc26e768"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[https://host.docker.internal:8111]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ul></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4b820ed4-59e2-4e23-afbc-a3b026c14629"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can apply the Magic Collaboration Studio license <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Applying Magic Collaboration Studio license" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[via the web UI]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <a href="https://osmc.nomagic.com/">REST API</a>. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="auto-cursor-target">Helpful commands</h3><p>To follow the output (tail) the output of a specific container:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a29d36fb-2a21-4e54-948e-19dbd878f536"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker compose logs -f <Container Name or ID>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To stop all containers but retain data in non-persistent storage:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="508d288c-bcad-481a-b504-59c8137d20ff"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker compose stop]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p> To stop all and remove all running containers (non-persistent storage data will be lost):</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a196ce33-51bc-4ee5-8b7a-b98e67e4e5f8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker compose down -v]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137991401 space=MCS2024x version=9 -->
## PAGE 00025: Building Web Application Platform containers

- page_id: `137991401`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137991401/Building+Web+Application+Platform+containers
- version_number: 9
- version_date: `2026-03-18T10:22:41.877+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Deployment Guide > Enterprise deployment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

do

not provide Docker support

https://docs.docker.com/

To build a Docker image, you need to create a Dockerfile. A Dockerfile is a file that contains instructions on how to build the Docker image. By default, the file is named *Dockerfile* and this naming is used in the instructions below. To learn more about Docker, see [https://docs.docker.com/get-started/](https://docs.docker.com/get-started/).

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

In addition to a Dockerfile, the following files are required to build the Docker image:

- <service_name>.war - a deployable archive of the desired web application, e.g., *webapp.war*, *collaborator.war*, or *admin.war*.
- logback.xml - the logging configuration file. To learn how to configure data logging, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Configuring data logging' space=''] .
- webappplatform.properties - the file with Web Application Platform configuration properties
- you may also need additional files, like email or document export templates that need to be copied to the web container.

Note that running multiple Docker images without resource limitation can consume too much of the host machine’s memory and cause "out of memory" kills. For more information on how to set resource constraints for a Docker container, refer to [https://docs.docker.com/config/containers/resource_constraints/](https://docs.docker.com/config/containers/resource_constraints/).

Here is a sample Dockerfile that can be used to build the Docker image:

```text
.war ${TOMCAT_DIR}/webapps/

RUN sed -i "s|shared\.loader=*$|shared\.loader=\"\${catalina.base}/shared/conf\"|" ${TOMCAT_DIR}/conf/catalina.properties && \
    echo 'export JAVA_OPTS="$JAVA_OPTS ${WEBAPP_PROPERTIES}"' >> ${SET_ENV_FILE} && \
    chmod o+x ${SET_ENV_FILE} && \
    groupadd --gid ${USER_GID} tomcat && \
    useradd -r --uid ${USER_UID} --gid ${USER_GID} -s /sbin/nologin -d ${TOMCAT_DIR} -c "Tomcat user" tomcat && \
    chown -R tomcat:tomcat ${TOMCAT_DIR} && \
    chown -R root:root ${TOMCAT_DIR}/bin/*.sh && \
    rm -rf webapps.dist

USER tomcat

EXPOSE 8080

CMD ["catalina.sh", "run"]]]>
```

For the correct version of Tomcat and JRE, please check the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''] page.

Instructions in the Dockerfile may be written in several ways depending on your particular needs and preferences. The table below lists script statements you can use with descriptions and the indication of whether the script part is required or optional.

| Script statement | Required or optional | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| FROM tomcat:x.x.x-jrexx-temurin-jammy | Required | In the FROM part where the base image needs to be specified, Tomcat should be selected as the base image and its version has to be compatible with the version of the web application code. |
| ARG TOMCAT_DIR=/usr/local/tomcat | Optional | An argument used as an intermediary variable to store the Tomcat directory location. The location may differ depending on a particular base image used in the FROM part. This argument is introduced for convenience since further instructions reference the Tomcat directory location multiple times. |
| ARG SET_ENV_FILE=/usr/local/tomcat/bin/setenv.sh | Optional | The path to the setenv.sh file which is used to set JAVA options. |
| ARG USER_UID=5000 | Required | The user ID that will be assigned to the Tomcat user (for Tomcat to be run as a Tomcat user and not a root). |
| ARG USER_GID=${USER_UID} | Required | An argument used to set a user group for the Tomcat user. |
| COPY logback.xml ${TOMCAT_DIR}/shared/conf/ | Optional (recommended) | Since Web Application Platform uses logback as the logging framework, there should be its configuration file available in the classpath. This command copies the logging configuration into the folder that will be included in the classpath.Note that it is possible to use other names for the configuration file. In this case, the script should be updated to reference another file. It is possible to omit the configuration file but it is not recommended. |
| COPY webappplatform.properties ${TOMCAT_DIR}/shared/conf/ | Optional | This command copies the properties file to the folder that is present in the classpath. If it is not copied, then an alternative solution must be used to reference the properties file. To learn about alternative options, see . |
| COPY <service_name>.war ${TOMCAT_DIR}/webapps/ | Required | This command copies the web application to the Tomcat deployment folder.Note that you can put multiple web applications in the same docker image. To do that, add a separate command for each .war file. |
| RUN sed -i "s\|shared\.loader=*$\|shared\.loader=\"\${catalina.base}/shared/conf\"\|" ${TOMCAT_DIR}/conf/catalina.properties && \ echo 'export JAVA_OPTS="$JAVA_OPTS ${WEBAPP_PROPERTIES}"' >> ${SET_ENV_FILE} && \ chmod o+x ${SET_ENV_FILE} && \ groupadd --gid ${USER_GID} tomcat && \ useradd -r --uid ${USER_UID} --gid ${USER_GID} -s /sbin/nologin -d ${TOMCAT_DIR} -c "Tomcat user" tomcat && \ chown -R tomcat:tomcat ${TOMCAT_DIR} && \ chown -R root:root ${TOMCAT_DIR}/bin/*.sh && \ rm -rf webapps.dist | Required if any additional files need to be in the classpath | This command updates the Tomcat configuration to include ${TOMCAT_DIR}/shared/conf in the classpath. In addition, it sets JAVA_OPTS and assigns permissions and a group to the Tomcat user. |
| USER tomcat | Required | Specify the user which will be used to start Tomcat. |
| EXPOSE 8080 | Required | The value should correspond to the one that is used by Tomcat to deploy the web application. You can use port 8080 or 8443 if SSL is needed. |
| CMD [“catalina.sh”, “run”] | Required | A command used to run the Tomcat server. |

Here is a sample command to build the Docker image:

```text
.]]>
```

The provided build command will work only if the Dockerfile script and all the files referenced in the script (in this case *<service_name>.war*, *logback.xml*, and *webappplatform.properties*) are stored in the current directory. To build Docker images using non-default file locations, consult the Docker documentation.

When a Docker image is successfully built, you can run the Docker container on port 8080 by executing the following command:

```text
]]>
```

````html
ARG USER_GID=${USER_UID}

COPY logback.xml ${TOMCAT_DIR}/shared/conf/
COPY webappplatform.properties ${TOMCAT_DIR}/shared/conf/

RUN sed -i "s|shared\.loader=*$|shared\.loader=\"\${catalina.base}/shared/conf\"|" ${TOMCAT_DIR}/conf/catalina.properties && \
    echo 'export JAVA_OPTS="$JAVA_OPTS ${WEBAPP_PROPERTIES}"' >> ${SET_ENV_FILE} && \

    groupadd --gid ${USER_GID} tomcat && \

    chown -R tomcat:tomcat ${TOMCAT_DIR} && \
    chown -R root:root ${TOMCAT_DIR}/bin/*.sh && \
    rm -rf webapps.dist

USER tomcat

EXPOSE 8080

CMD ["catalina.sh", "run"]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d0093cdc-9c14-405a-aaef-a072f3178eb1"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For the correct version of Tomcat and JRE, please check the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Third-Party Component Compatibility" /></ac:link> page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><br />Instructions in the Dockerfile may be written in several ways depending on your particular needs and preferences. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137992319 space=MCS2024x version=4 -->
````
## PAGE 00027: Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud

- page_id: `137992319`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137992319/Cameo+Collaborator+for+Teamwork+Cloud
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2024-07-03T09:09:40.486+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > User Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud is a web-based product designed to present models in a simplified form for stakeholders, sponsors, customers, and engineering teams. It allows editing and reviewing models. The product is built with scalability in mind, making it possible to deploy it both in a single node and a cluster setting. It works in all form factors (desktop, tablet, and smart phones), making model sharing and reviewing easy.

Watch the following video to see Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud in action:

800450

Use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud to:

- Present models to stakeholders, sponsors, or customers.
- Present models to engineering teams (modelers and non-modelers) within your organization.
- Keep stakeholders and engineering teams up-to-date with the precise, consistent information from the single source of truth - model repository.
- Review models and write comments to provide feedback to model authors.
- Address comments by making changes to models without using a modeling tool.
- Edit models on the web without having to use a modeling tool, e.g., for specification of interface libraries or editing requirements

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The conceptual schema of Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud.

#### NOTE: Cameo Collaborator and 3DEXPERIENCE platform

Cameo Collaborator and 3DEXPERIENCE platform

If [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Enabling 3DEXPERIENCE collaboration in a modeling tool' space='MD2024x'], The Cameo Collaborator Publisher plugin is disabled in the modeling tool, and you cannot work with Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud.

To learn how to install an use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud, refer to the following topics:

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud is a web-based product designed to present models in a simplified form for stakeholders, sponsors, customers, and engineering teams. It allows editing and reviewing models. The product is built with scalability in mind, making it possible to deploy it both in a single node and a cluster setting. It works in all form factors (desktop, tablet, and smart phones), making model sharing and reviewing easy.</p><p style="text-align: left;">Watch the following video to see Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud in action:<br /><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="41338db9-c745-4d83-a11f-a0eb646f7bcd"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">800</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YE6GCoJVHeg" /></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="height">450</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p class="bodytext" style="text-align: left;">Use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud to:</p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li><p>Present models to stakeholders, sponsors, or customers.</p></li><li><p>Present models to engineering teams (modelers and non-modelers) within your organization.</p></li><li><p>Keep stakeholders and engineering teams up-to-date with the precise, consistent information from the single source of truth - model repository.</p></li><li><p>Review models and write comments to provide feedback to model authors.</p></li><li><p>Address comments by making changes to models without using a modeling tool.</p></li><li><p>Edit models on the web without having to use a modeling tool, e.g., for specification of interface libraries or editing requirements</p></li></ul><p style="text-align: center;"><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="high_level_architecture.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The conceptual schema of Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d91ba1c8-fbaf-4f6b-8bc8-b511516d007b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Cameo Collaborator and 3DEXPERIENCE platform</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">If <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Enabling 3DEXPERIENCE collaboration in a modeling tool" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[collaboration powered by the 3DEXPERIENCE platform is enabled in the Environment Options]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, The Cameo Collaborator Publisher plugin is disabled in the modeling tool, and you cannot work with Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To learn how to install an use Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud, refer to the following topics:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="30f8fb65-235d-42cb-b6c3-4f6e498ffe87" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137992009 space=MCS2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00029: Case study

- page_id: `137992009`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137992009/Case+study
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-12-02T16:42:49.626+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > User Guide > Admin web applications > Roles application > Role-Based Access Control concept
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This case study presents a simple situation in an organization where users are classified into two groups: Team A and Team B according to their type. Team A is a group of users imported from an LDAP server and Team B is a group of internal users created using Teamwork Cloud Admin. This case study is used to illustrate how roles are assigned to each user in each team to work on different projects.

The following figure illustrates the two groups of users in the organization, Team A and Team B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The users are grouped into Team A (external users) and Team B (internal users).

There are three projects for the teams to work on: Project A, Project B, and Project C.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Project A is assigned to Team A, Project B is assigned to Team B, and Project C is assigned to both Team A and Team B.

Before starting to work on the projects, the organization needs to create a proper role and scope for everyone in the teams so that it can divide the tasks among them. The following figures show the team members' roles in Project A and Project B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A specific role is assigned to each member in Team A in Project A and Team B in Project B.

Since Project C is the largest, the organization decided to assign it to both Team A and Team B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Besides working on their respective project, both teams are also responsible for Project C.

More roles are created to manage the team members and the system. The following figure, for example, shows the user u7's roles as both User Manager and Security Manager, and the u8's role as a Project Creator. The user u7's role is to manage all users and access so the right scope to assign is **Global**. The user u8's role is to create projects so the right scope to assign is also **Global**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The role scope of the user u7 and user u8 is Global.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This case study presents a simple situation in an organization where users are classified into two groups: Team A and Team B according to their type. Team A is a group of users imported from an LDAP server and Team B is a group of internal users created using Teamwork Cloud Admin. This case study is used to illustrate how roles are assigned to each user in each team to work on different projects.</p><p>The following figure illustrates the two groups of users in the organization, Team A and Team B.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy1.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The users are grouped into Team A (external users) and Team B (internal users).</h6><p>There are three projects for the teams to work on: Project A, Project B, and Project C. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy2.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Project A is assigned to Team A, Project B is assigned to Team B, and Project C is assigned to both Team A and Team B.</h6><p>Before starting to work on the projects, the organization needs to create a proper role and scope for everyone in the teams so that it can divide the tasks among them. The following figures show the team members' roles in Project A and Project B.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy3.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A specific role is <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="e16e11b2-61b0-441f-9713-b4f80ab5c5a7">assigned to</ac:inline-comment-marker> each member in Team A in Project A and Team B in Project B.</h6><p>Since Project C is the largest, the organization decided to assign it to both Team A and Team B.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy4.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Besides <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="6005609b-48cb-43d2-a005-f1ff1da66866">working</ac:inline-comment-marker> on <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="2627d052-08a1-4746-bc2a-267806252fea">their resp</ac:inline-comment-marker>ective project, both teams are also responsible for Project C.</h6><p>More roles are created to manage the team members and the system. The following figure, for example, shows the user u7's roles as both User Manager and Security Manager, and the u8's role as a Project Creator. The user u7's role is to manage all users and access so the right scope to assign is <strong>Global</strong>. The user u8's role is to create projects so the right scope to assign is also <strong>Global</strong>.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="casestudy5.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="fa4fedb2-493c-4085-930a-c47d8bbf3d8f">The</ac:inline-comment-marker> role scope of the user u7 and <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="54648af0-6f12-421c-96a1-0fc51b0d8e8d">user</ac:inline-comment-marker> u8 is Global.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137991333 space=MCS2024x version=4 -->
## PAGE 00030: Cassandra and Magic Collaboration Studio cluster setup

- page_id: `137991333`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137991333/Cassandra+and+Magic+Collaboration+Studio+cluster+setup
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-12-11T13:41:58.392+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Deployment Guide > Magic Collaboration Studio Installation > Advanced Magic Collaboration Studio installation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

5

This page provides instructions to set up a Magic Collaboration Studio cluster on your system. A Magic Collaboration Studio cluster is composed of two clustering layers – Magic Collaboration Studio and Cassandra.

To set up a Magic Collaboration Studio cluster, you need to perform two separate tasks:

1. 
2. 

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An illustration of Magic Collaboration Studio cluster and Cassandra cluster nodes within Magic Collaboration Studio cluster.

##### Setting up a Cassandra cluster

Prior to establishing a Cassandra cluster, you need to determine the following.

- The initial number of nodes in the cluster.
- The IP address of each node.
- Determine which node will be the contact point.

The minimum recommended cluster size for Cassandra is 3 nodes, when taking availability and redundancy into account. Cassandra's default read and write consistency is quorum. Replication factor should match the minimum node count of 3. It is recommended to scale up a cluster in multiples of 3.

When setting up a Cassandra node, you need to configure a seed that indicates an initial contact point for the new node. So, you can configure the contact point using the IP of any existing active node in the cluster. Use the node's own IP when configuring the first node. For example, if you are configuring a three-node cluster and the IPs of the nodes are *10.1.1.101*, *10.1.1.102,* and *10.1.1.103,* select one of them (e.g. *10.1.1.101*) as the **seed**. When configuring **cassandra.yaml**, specify *10.1.1.101* as the contact point value for all of the three nodes.

| Machine IP | 10.1.1.101 | 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.103 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| seeds | 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102 |
| listen_address | 10.1.1.101 | 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.103 |
| broadcast_rpc_address | 10.1.1.101 | 10.1.1.102 | 10.1.1.103 |

Note that

listen_address

and

broadcast_rpc_address

are still set to their respective machine’s address

Now that you understand what a seed is and how to configure it, follow the instructions to install and configure Cassandra on Windows or Linux operating system.

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing Cassandra on Windows' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing and configuring Cassandra on Linux' space='TWCloud2024x']

After installing and configuring all nodes, start the seed node. Verify that it is operational by issuing the command "nodetool status".

Once the seed node is operational, you can proceed to start the other nodes in the cluster. You must allow the node to join the cluster before proceeding to the next node. After all nodes have started, the "nodetool status" command will display results as shown below.

```text

```

#### NOTE: Node Consistency

Node Consistency

In order to ensure that all nodes are consistent, please run the "nodetool repair" command nightly. This can be scheduled as a crontab job or via Windows Task Scheduler. This only needs to be executed on a single node.

###### **Cassandra configuration parameters**

*-***esi.persistence.datastax-java-driver.basic.contact-points**

The value of this parameter is a list of Cassandra node IP addresses. You can find this parameter in **application.conf**by looking for the following.

```text

```

*-***esi.persistence.cassandra.keyspace.replication-factor**

This parameter defines the Cassandra replication factor which describes how many copies of your data will be written by Cassandra. For example, replication factor 2 means your data will be written to 2 nodes.

For a three-node cluster, if you would like the cluster to be able to survive 1 node loss, you will need to set the replication factor to 3.

```text

```

Please note that this configuration will be used only for the first time Magic Collaboration Studio connects to Cassandra and creates a new twc keyspace. Changing the replication factor after the keyspace has already been created is rather a complex task. Read [this document](https://docs.datastax.com/en/cql/3.3/cql/cql_using/useUpdateKeyspaceRF.html) if you need to change it.

Magic Collaboration Studio uses QUORUM for both write and read consistency levels.

[Click here](https://docs.datastax.com/en/cassandra/3.0/cassandra/dml/dmlAboutDataConsistency.html) for a detailed explanation of data consistency.

**Update Authentication Server parameters**

Apply to**authserver.properties.**

```perl

```

To start up the Magic Collaboration Studio cluster, start the server on the seed machine and wait until you see a message similar to the following in the **server.log**.

```text

```

Then you can start the server on the remaining machines. You should see the following messages in the **server.log** which shows all 3 nodes are forming the cluster.

```text

```

##### Setting up a Magic Collaboration Studio cluster

Before setting up a Magic Collaboration Studio cluster, you need to determine the following.

- The initial number of nodes in the cluster.
- The IP address of each Magic Collaboration Studio node.
- Prepare a list of IPs of Cassandra nodes.

The instructions below use the following sample environment for ease of understanding.

- 3 nodes Magic Collaboration Studio cluster.
- Magic Collaboration Studio node IP addresses are 10.1.1.111, 10.1.1.112 , and 10.1.1.113.
- All nodes will be used as seed nodes.
- IP addresses of Cassandra nodes are 10.1.1.101, 10.1.1.102, 10.1.1.103.

Setting up the Magic Collaboration Studio cluster involves 2 groups of parameters in **application.conf**.

###### **Magic Collaboration Studio clustering parameter**

*-***akka.cluster.seed-nodes**

This parameter indicates the initial seed for the cluster.

If you install Magic Collaboration Studio using the installer file, you will be asked to provide the seed node IP during installation process and the value will be configured in **application.conf**.

If you install using the zip file, you will need to manually configure the parameter in****application.conf**. Search for the following:

```text

```

Replace *${seed-node.ip}*with the IP addresses of the seed nodes, so it should look similar to the following:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="40c9b063-9207-4f03-8620-076bf13c3be7"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">5</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>This page provides instructions to set up a Magic Collaboration Studio cluster on your system.
````
