All of the data centers would form one Cassandra cluster, and all of them would have access to the latest data.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">In this scenario, performance would mainly be affected by the database write operations. Write operations would be applied to all data centers. Read operations would remain local and thus not affect performance.</span></p><h3 style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><strong>Deploying Multi-DC Cassandra for Magic Collaboration Studio</strong></span></h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">This is the recommended configuration procedure for deploying Multi-DC Cassandra for Magic Collaboration Studio.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">To configure Multi-DC Cassandra cluster</span></p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring Cassandra cluster with multiple data centers"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Configure Cassandra cluster with more than one data center.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li>Start Cassandra cluster and verify all data centers are online.</li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring Magic Collaboration Studio for Multi-DC"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Configure Magic Collaboration Studio for Multi-DC.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring authentication in Web Application Platform for Multi-DC"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Configure authentication in Web Application Platform for Multi-DC.]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">For an existing database</span>, <ac:link ac:anchor="Configuring Cassandra keyspace"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[update the “twc” keyspace for Multi-DC setup]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Start Magic Collaboration Studio services and verify operation.</span></li></ol><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><strong>Configuring Cassandra cluster with multiple data centers</strong></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Our example deployment is a single Cassandra cluster with two data centers located in separate geographical locations. Each Cassandra data center has three nodes. When performing the initial installation and configuration of Cassandra nodes, make sure the version of Cassandra is consistent across all nodes. Also, make sure to synchronize clocks across all nodes. </span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Start by updating the <em>cassandra.yaml</em> configuration file.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Set a unique cluster name for all nodes. This deployment is a single cluster spanning multiple data centers. The cluster name should be the same across all nodes.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="74e767c7-e04a-4920-9421-eb5ba65a2644"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[cluster_name: 'MDC Cluster']]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Set the seed provider for all data centers. Assign at least two seed nodes for each data center. Specify the address for all seeds. Alternatively, use a seed provider service to keep track of seed node addresses.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9f21423e-fe82-4b53-b0a4-2354e7fe0f05"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[seed_provider:
````html
    - class_name: org.apache.cassandra.locator.SimpleSeedProvider
      parameters:
          - seeds: "10.0.0.1, 10.0.0.2, 10.0.1.1, 10.0.1.2"]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Then, change the endpoint snitch implementation on each node. The default snitch is <em>SimpleSnitch</em>, which does not support Multi-DC. Cassandra uses snitch to gather network topology information. In this case, we are using <em>GossipingPropertyFileSnitch</em> for Multi-DC setup. Cassandra will obtain data center information from the <em>cassandra-rackdc.properties</em> file when <em>GossipingPropertyFileSnitch</em> is set.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fc269aa0-2fed-430b-a612-84d77f41ce2c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[endpoint_snitch: GossipingPropertyFileSnitch]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Refer to Cassandra documentation if you are updating snitch for an exiting database: <a style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-blue-bolder,#0c66e4);" href="https://docs.datastax.com/en/cassandra-oss/3.0/cassandra/operations/opsSwitchSnitch.html">https://docs.datastax.com/en/cassandra-oss/3.0/cassandra/operations/opsSwitchSnitch.html</a></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Edit the <em>cassandra-rackdc.properties</em> file for each node. All nodes in a data center should have the same “dc” name.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">For the primary data center:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2f9ec96e-2868-4b44-99ed-f47bc4134781"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[dc=datacenter1
rack=rack1]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">For the backup data center:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ec0d8678-6728-4ff6-99b5-3e3aa70a6088"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[dc=datacenter2
rack=rack1]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Start each Cassandra node. Port 7000 is the default inter-node communication (encrypted and unencrypted) port for a Cassandra cluster. Make sure port 7000 is open for communication between data centers. Please note that the initial connection between data centers can take some time. Run <em>nodetool status</em> to confirm that all data centers have connected to the cluster.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">For an existing Cassandra cluster, run <em>nodetool repair</em> and <em>nodetool cleanup</em> after the above configurations have been made.</span></p><h4 style="line-height: 1.42857;color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><strong>Configuring Magic Collaboration Studio for Multi-DC</strong></span></h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Once all Cassandra nodes are online and data center connections are confirmed, configure the Magic Collaboration Studio <em>application.conf</em> file for Multi-DC.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">In the <em>esi.persistence.cassandra.keyspace</em> section, we have added the following parameters for our example. With 3 nodes in each data center, the replication factor is set to 3. The consistency level is set to “FAST_RELIABILITY_GLOBAL&quot; for a disaster recovery scenario. Please refer to the table below for consistency setting options. If deploying an entirely new database, the “twc” keyspace will be created with the replication strategy and factors specified here. For an existing database, you will have to manually edit the keyspace replication parameters (see Cassandra Keyspace Configuration below).</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6c8b0f69-b6b7-4080-b030-dccd9259860e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[is-multi-dc = true
strategy-class = "NetworkTopologyStrategy"
consistency = "FAST_RELIABILITY_GLOBAL"
dc-replication-factors {
     datacenter1=3
     datacenter2=3
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">When <em>is-multi-dc</em> is set to <em>true</em>, the <em>replication-factor</em> parameter is ignored.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">The <em>dc-replication-factors</em> configuration will only be applied when creating a new “twc” keyspace. If there is an existing “twc” keyspace in Cassandra, Magic Collaboration Studio will use the existing replication factor set in the database. Use <em>ALTER KEYSPACE</em> to change the replication factor.</span></li></ul><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th style="background-color: rgb(241,242,244);"><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><strong>Keyspace Consistency Setting</strong></span></p></th><th style="background-color: rgb(241,242,244);"><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><strong>Write Consistency Level</strong></span></p></th><th style="background-color: rgb(241,242,244);"><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><strong>Read Consistency Level</strong></span></p></th></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">BEST_READ_PERFORMANCE_GLOBAL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">ALL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">ONE</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">BEST_READ_PERFORMANCE_LOCAL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">ALL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">LOCAL_ONE</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">BEST_RELIABILITY_GLOBAL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">EACH_QUORUM</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">EACH_QUORUM</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">FAST_RELIABILITY_GLOBAL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">EACH_QUORUM</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">LOCAL_QUORUM</span></p></td></tr><tr><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">BEST_RELIABILITY_LOCAL</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">LOCAL_QUORUM</span></p></td><td><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">LOCAL_QUORUM</span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">In the <em>esi.persistence.datastax-java-driver.basic</em> section, set the data center name and contact points to local values. The local data center will be used as the primary database for client requests.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e5434990-4ea4-4c49-91c3-f17b93684643"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[load-balancing-policy.local-datacenter = datacenter1]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Use the seed nodes from the local data center specified in <em>cassandra.yaml</em></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="882ea537-923b-4bdf-86fd-cb7e8f683dee"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[contact-points = ["10.0.0.1:9042","10.0.0.2:9042"]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4 style="line-height: 1.42857;color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><strong>Configuring authentication in Web Application Platform for Multi-DC</strong></span></h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">The Authentication web app will need to be configured to use the local data center (<em>datacenter1</em> in this example). Add the <em>cassandra.localDatacenter</em> parameter in <em>authserver.properties</em>. Update <em>cassandra.contactPoints</em> to local Cassandra node addresses, if needed.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1ccd756a-4b32-4a24-8254-7df75d0f4cb8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[cassandra.localDatacenter=datacenter1]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4 style="line-height: 1.42857;color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><strong>Configuring Cassandra keyspace</strong></span></h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">If you are deploying Multi-DC with an existing Cassandra database, you will need to manually reconfigure the replication parameters for the “twc” keyspace.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Use <em>cqlsh</em>, the Cassandra command-line interface, to modify “twc” keyspace replication parameters.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7e609f3d-bc25-49be-afb6-538060dec874"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ALTER KEYSPACE twc WITH replication = {'class': 'NetworkTopologyStrategy', 'datacenter1' : 3, 'datacenter2' : 3};]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Run <em>nodetool repair</em> after keyspace modification to update all nodes.</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">To verify replication in <em>cqlsh</em>:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fd6ba8d6-7cf0-4045-a8cd-b57634c6bb58"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[select * from system_schema.keyspaces;]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">These steps can also be used to create the “twc” keyspace. Note that manual keyspace creation is not needed, as Magic Collaboration Studio will create the keyspace during initial startup.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7c7f50b5-4d1c-4b90-90f0-d972fed3e78a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="linenumbers">true</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[CREATE KEYSPACE twc WITH replication = {'class': 'NetworkTopologyStrategy', 'datacenter1' : 3, 'datacenter2' : 3}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><strong>Multi-DC Cassandra Best Practices</strong></span></h3><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">The Cassandra version must be the same across all nodes and data centers</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">At least two seed nodes should be assigned for each data center</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">Clocks should be synchronized for all node</span></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=158598137 space=MCS2024x version=3 -->
## PAGE 00074: Deployment example for Web Application Platform on Kubernetes

- page_id: `158598137`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/158598137/Deployment+example+for+Web+Application+Platform+on+Kubernetes
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2026-02-10T14:43:16.884+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Deployment Guide > Enterprise deployment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This deployment example is not intended for production use. Use it for testing purposes only.

This chapter provides information on how to deploy the product for testing purposes. Keep in mind that the guidelines provided below are not considered the best practices and do not take into account security settings and password encryption. This deployment has been tested with a specific Kubernetes version, CNI, container runtime, and OS. If you use other solutions, use them at your own risk.

#### NOTE: Prerequisites

Prerequisites

- Kubernetes cluster (for more information, refer to https://kubernetes.io/ ):
  - 1 virtual machine used as the control plane node and 3 nodes used as worker nodes
  - Control plane node: 4 CPU cores, 8GB RAM
  - Worker node: 4 CPU cores, 32GB RAM
- Kubernetes version: v1.25.5
- Container runtime: Containerd (for more information, refer to [https://containerd.io/](https://containerd.io/))
- CNI: Calico
- OS: Red Hat Enterprise Linux release 8.7 (Ootpa)
- Load Balancer: MetalLB
- IP: 1 IP address with the DNS A record that points to this IP (IP from your network that is not used by your cluster nodes).
- Ingress: ingress-nginx (with sticky session support). Note that there are two nginx ingresses: ingress-nginx is maintained by the Kubernetes community, while nginx-ingress is maintained by F5 inc. The free F5 inc. version of nginx-ingress does not support “sticky sessions” which are required. Therefore, make sure you use ingress-nginx.
- A Host with Docker or Podman to build images and push them to the image registry.
- An image registry (repository) such as Harbor, Sonatype Nexus, Gitlab, or Docker registry server to push and pull the images you will build.
- Teamwork Cloud server
- The applications needed to run Web Application Platform services:
  - Zookeeper (for more information, refer to [https://zookeeper.apache.org/](https://zookeeper.apache.org/))
  - Apache Artemis MQ (for more information, refer to [https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/](https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/))
  - Keda CRD (deployed with the Simulation web application) See the compatible versions of the applications in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''] page.

The following figure displays the environment used in this deployment example.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### In this environment, the K8S cluster admin host (Linux virtual machine) is used to build images, run the image repository, and manage the Kubernetes cluster.

In the workflow below:

- admin.example.com refers to the K8S cluster admin host and should be changed to your K8S cluster admin FQDN.
- webapp.example.com refers to the domain name through which Web Application Platform will be accessible.
- All the steps are performed on the K8S cluster admin host.

To deploy the product for testing purposes

1. Download the *[ATTACHMENT filename='webapp-charts.zip']* file.
2. Install kubectl as described at https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/install-kubectl-linux/ .
3. Install the helm to the K8S cluster admin host as described at https://helm.sh/docs/intro/install .
4. Execute the following command to copy cluster config files to the K8S cluster admin host: :.kube ~
Edit ~/.kube/config]]> Replace the localhost IP address with your control plane IP address or hostname, e.g.,*https://127.0.0.1:6443*.
5. Build Docker images:
  1. Prepare the required files and directories.
  2. Download the installation packag e for your product version. For more information, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Downloading installation files' space='NMDOC'] .
  3. Extract the *webapp-charts.zip* file, and inside the *webapp-charts root* directory, extract the *Web_App_Platform_<version>_linux_no_install.zip*file. After extracting the files, you should have the following folder structure: *<webapp-charts_root>/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/*. You will find all the files mentioned in this chapter in *Web_App_Platform_<version>_linux_no_install.zip* or on the software download website (except for Teamwork Cloud keystore and SSL certificate):The *<webapp-charts_root>/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps* directory contains application .war files.The *<webapp-charts_root>/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf* directory contains other configuration files.The *<webapp-charts_root>/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/conf* directory contains *server.xml*.
6. Use the same SSL certificate and key that were used for the Teamwork Cloud server. You can copy the key and certificate files from the Teamwork Cloud server.
7. If you use only the .p12 file on the Teamwork Cloud server, execute the following command to extract**the certificate and keyfrom the .p12 file:
8. Execute the following command to create a directory for each application you are going to use:
9. Copy all files listed below to their directories (except the *artemis* directory). You will find the Docker files for each service in *[ATTACHMENT filename='dockerfiles.zip'].* After placing the Docker files in their appropriate directories, rename them to *Dockerfile*as shown below: admin-console*admin.war**Dockerfile*authentication*authentication.war**Dockerfile*collaborator*collaborator.war**Dockerfile**data* (The directory is located in *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/*. Before copying, remove the *document-exporter* directory from the *data* directory.)document-exporter*Dockerfile**data* (The directory is located in *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/*. Before copying, remove the *collaborator* directory from the *data* directory.)*document-exporter.war**collaborator.war*oslc*collaborator.war**Dockerfile*reports*Dockerfile**reports.war*resource-usage-map*Dockerfile**resource-usage-map.war*resources*Dockerfile**resources.war*simulation*Dockerfile**simulation.war*webapp*Dockerfile**webapp.war*
10. Execute the following command to start the local Docker registry: In this case, a local Docker registry is run on the K8S cluster admin host. It uses port 5000, so make sure this port is open on your host firewall and accessible to your Kubernetes cluster. This local Docker registry should be used for testing purposes only and is not considered production-ready. In the production environment, run the image registry with TLS and authentication.
11. Add your repo URL (FQDN of the K8S cluster admin host) to the Docker /etc/docker/daemon.json file.
12. If the *daemon.json*file does not exist, create it. Assuming there are no other settings in the file, it should have the following content:
13. Execute the following command to restart the Docker service:
14. If the registry container has started, proceed with building the Artemis image (for more information, refer to [https://github.com/apache/activemq-artemis/tree/main/artemis-docker](https://github.com/apache/activemq-artemis/tree/main/artemis-docker)):
  1. Execute the following command:
  2. Execute the following command to download Artemis binaries:
  3. Extract the following file:
  4. Execute the following commands to build the image:
  5. Execute the following command to tag the built image:
  6. Execute the following command to push the image to your repository:
15. Execute the commands below for each service that you want to build images for inside their own directories, as shown in the examples in steps 15a, 15b, and 15c. {IMAGE_REPO_URL} - your image repository URL. It can differ depending on the image registry provider.{APP_NAME} - application name.{VERSION} - version to be used for the tag.
  1. The following command example builds images for Admin Console:
  2. The following command example tags the built image:
  3. The following command example pushes the image to the registry:
16. To be able to pull images to your Kubernetes cluster nodes, add your image repository to the Containerd configuration on all cluster nodes .
17. Edit the */etc/containerd/config.toml* file by adding the following lines to the "mirrors" part: Keep in mind that alignment is very important, as shown in the example below.[IMAGE alt='' src='']
18. Execute the following command to restart Containerd services on all cluster nodes:
19. Execute the following commands to add dependency repos:
20. Execute the following command to update chart dependencies:
21. Execute the command below to install Custom Resource Definitions (CRD). This will create all services and deployments. If you use a later Kubernetes version than v1.26, change the keda version in the command to 2.9.0You can check the status of the services and pods with this command: kubectl get all Keda and MetalLB should be added as separate resources.Since CRDs (being a globally shared resource) are fragile, you have to assume that it is shared across multiple namespaces and groups of users once a CRD is installed. For that reason, installing, modifying, and deleting CRDs is a process that has ramifications for all users and systems of that cluster.
22. Execute the following command to deploy MetalLB:
23. To check the MetalLB deployment status, execute the following command on the control plane node or the K8S cluster admin host: You should see an output similar to this: 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] If you see that the MetalLB status is 'Running,' but READY is 0/1, give it some time to start and repeat the command.
24. Create configuration files for MetalLB: Make sure to complete all the substeps of this step to create and apply both configurations. Otherwise, the product will not work properly.
  1. Go the the directory where you extracted the *webapp-charts.zip* file: ]]>
  2. Edit the*metalLB/metallb_ipadresspool.yaml* file as shown below: Your IT department should give you a reserved IP address or a range of IP addresses (depending on your needs) with a DNS A record that points to this IP address.Configuration files should be formatted in *.yaml*.
  3. Edit the crd/*metalLB/l2advertisement.yaml* file as shown below. You can change the metadata name as needed. Keep in mind that the ipAddressPool name should match the specifications.
  4. Execute the following command to apply the MetalLB configuration:
  5. Execute the following command to check the applied configuration:
  6. Execute the following command to create the MetalLB advertisement:
  7. Execute the following command to check the advertisement configuration:
25. Execute the commands below to collect configuration files for all applications and copy them to their directories. These files will be used to create config maps and mount them into pods. Make sure that you work in the directory where you extracted the *webapp-charts.zip* file (<webapp-charts_root> directory). /CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/authserver.properties configs/auth
cp /CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/authserver.properties configs/logback.xml revision.txt webappplatform.properties configs/wap
cp /CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/webappplatform.properties configs/wap
cp /CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/ logback.xml configs/wap
cp /CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/revision.txt configs/wap
cp /CATIANoMagicServices/conf/catalina.properties server.xml configs/tomcat
/keystore.p12 configs/twcloud
cp /teamworkcloud.crt configs/twcloud]]>
26. In the *webapp-charts root* directory, find the v alues.yaml file and provide the values of the parameters inside the file to enable or disable specific applications or parts of the configuration.
27. Do one of the following:
  - Execute this command in the *webapp-charts root* directory to deploy services to the default namespace:
  - Execute this command in the helm parent chart directory to create a namespace and deploy services in this namespace: This helm chart includes Zookeeper and Ingress-nginx as dependencies. They will be deployed automatically.
28. After all web applications are deployed, execute the following command to check their status: All pods should be READY:1/1, STATUS:Running.The ingress-nginx service should be Type:LoadBalancer.EXTERNAL-IP should match the address that you provided in the MetalLB configuration.If pods do not run, check for problems by executing this command:kubectl describe pod pod_name
29. Execute the following command to check Ingress rules: You should get an output similar to this: )
TLS:
 webapp-tls-secret terminates nm-wap10639.dsone.3ds.com
Rules:
 Host Path Backends
 ---- ---- --------
 nm-wap10639.dsone.3ds.com
 /admin webapp-adminconsole:8443 (10.233.105.39:8443)
 /authentication webapp-authentication:8443 (10.233.88.69:8443)
 /collaborator webapp-collaborator:8443 (10.233.88.75:8443)
 /document-exporter webapp-docexporter:8443 (10.233.105.22:8443)
 /oslc webapp-oslc:8443 (10.233.105.23:8443)
 /reports webapp-reports:8443 (10.233.73.246:8443)
 /resources webapp-resources:8443 (10.233.88.67:8443)
 /resource-usage-map webapp-rum:8443 (10.233.105.40:8443)
 /simulation webapp-simulation:8443 (10.233.88.123:8443)
 /webapp webapp-webapp:8443 (10.233.105.29:8443)
Annotations: meta.helm.sh/release-name: webapp
 meta.helm.sh/release-namespace: default
 nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/affinity: cookie
 nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/affinity-canary-behavior: sticky
 nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/affinity-mode: persistent
 nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/backend-protocol: https
 nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size: 100m
 nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-connect-timeout: 600
 nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-read-timeout: 600
 nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-send-timeout: 600
 nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/send-timeout: 600
 nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/session-cookie-name: COOKIE
Events: ]]>
30. Test the product deployment:
  1. In an internet browser, go to *domain_name/webapp* (the DNS A record that points to the Ingress external IP). If you added a DNS record to bind the domain name to the IP address, you can use the domain name instead of the IP. The browser should show a warning because of the self-signed certificate. Accept it to proceed, and you will be redirected to the Teamwork Cloud Authentication web page. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  2. Log in with your credentials, and you will be redirected back to Web Application Platform .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ffbfdc68-df46-41ad-b222-3044a3aca6a3"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This deployment example is not intended for production use. Use it for testing purposes only.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br />This chapter provides information on how to deploy the product for testing purposes. Keep in mind that the guidelines provided below are not considered the best practices and do not take into account security settings and password encryption. This deployment has been tested with a specific Kubernetes version, CNI, container runtime, and OS. If you use other solutions, use them at your own risk.</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="25fec92e-70f2-40c3-94aa-0b5337dcef0b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Prerequisites</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="433d8a01-7e02-4f48-90d1-84ef787c8cea">Kubernetes</ac:inline-comment-marker> cluster (for more information, refer to <a href="https://kubernetes.io/">https://kubernetes.io/</a>):<ul><li>1 virtual machine used as the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="3094a6e3-4a79-4c59-89a8-f7a3c435a02f">control plane</ac:inline-comment-marker> node and 3 nodes used as worker nodes</li><li>Control plane node: 4 CPU cores, 8GB RAM</li><li>Worker node: 4 CPU cores, 32GB RAM</li></ul></li><li>Kubernetes version: v1.25.5</li><li>Container runtime: <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="1427fa05-c5a1-4a9f-9e6b-78a9b067b785">Containerd (for more information, refer to <a href="https://containerd.io/">https://containerd.io/</a>)</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li>CNI: Calico</li><li>OS: Red Hat Enterprise Linux release 8.7 (Ootpa)</li><li>Load Balancer: MetalLB</li><li>IP: 1 IP address with the DNS A record that points to this IP (IP from your network that is not used by your cluster nodes).</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Ingress: ingress-nginx (with sticky session support).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3b1109ce-5a64-41c4-a347-fd843dd27752"><ac:rich-text-body>Note that there are two nginx ingresses: ingress-nginx is maintained by the Kubernetes community, while nginx-ingress is maintained by F5 inc. The free F5 inc. version of nginx-ingress does not support “sticky sessions” which are required. Therefore, make sure you use ingress-nginx.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>A Host with Docker or Podman to build images and push them to the image registry.</li><li>An image registry (repository) such as Harbor, Sonatype Nexus, Gitlab, or Docker registry server to push and pull the images you will build.</li><li>Teamwork Cloud server</li><li>The applications needed to run Web Application Platform services:<ul><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a468635f-cead-49f6-813a-d15bbfe26480">Zookeeper (for more information, refer to <a href="https://zookeeper.apache.org/">https://zookeeper.apache.org/</a>)</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Apache Artemis MQ (for more information, refer to <a href="https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/">https://activemq.apache.org/components/artemis/</a>)</span></li><li>Keda CRD (deployed with the Simulation web application)<br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">See the compatible versions of the applications in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Third-Party Component Compatibility" /></ac:link> page.</span></li></ul></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br />The following figure displays the environment used in this deployment <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="4c979ac6-97ce-494a-8bb3-fc03c80e576f">example.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="environment_example.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"> In this environment, the K8S cluster admin host (Linux virtual machine) is used to build images, run <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="973f825f-ea11-431a-ba3d-dae565e8a69b">the image repository</ac:inline-comment-marker>, and manage the Kubernetes cluster.</h6><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4c011d33-341d-48fd-9c80-eb4eb5be3136"><ac:rich-text-body><p>In the workflow below:</p><ul><li><em>admin.example.com</em> refers to the K8S cluster admin host and should be changed to your K8S cluster admin FQDN.</li><li><em>webapp.example.com</em> refers to the domain name through which Web Application Platform will be accessible.</li><li>All the steps are performed on the <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">K8S cluster admin host.</span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To deploy the product for testing purposes</p><hr /><ol><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b1e13e7f-9985-4a4e-ad48-1c41d5a68cb8">Download the <em><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="webapp-charts.zip" /></ac:link></em> file.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li>Install kubectl as described at <a href="https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/install-kubectl-linux/">https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/install-kubectl-linux/</a><span>.</span></li><li>Install the helm to the K8S cluster admin host as described at <a href="https://helm.sh/docs/intro/install">https://helm.sh/docs/intro/install</a>.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the following command to copy cluster config files to the K8S cluster admin host:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="717f3d90-3f35-4911-8a61-168ef5158cc8"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[scp -r <user@control_plane_address>:.kube ~
Edit ~/.kube/config]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="071cbd84-9e14-45d4-b91b-941e37026c5a"><ac:rich-text-body><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="09f221f4-a2d2-4714-8415-cab0dd653b37"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Replace the localhost IP address with your control plane IP address or hostname</span>, e.g., </ac:inline-comment-marker><em><span class="nolink"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="09f221f4-a2d2-4714-8415-cab0dd653b37">https://127.0.0.1:6443</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="09f221f4-a2d2-4714-8415-cab0dd653b37">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Build Docker images:</p><ol><li>Prepare the required files and directories.</li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7b58ed06-d94e-4898-a035-019e24c67ffa">Download the installation packag</ac:inline-comment-marker>e for your product version. For more information, refer to <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="NMDOC" ri:content-title="Downloading installation files" /></ac:link>.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Extract the <em>webapp-charts.zip</em> file, and inside the <em>webapp-charts root</em> directory, extract the <em>Web_App_Platform_&lt;version&gt;_linux_no_install.zip </em>file. After extracting the files, you should have the following folder structure: <em>&lt;webapp-charts_root&gt;/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e80c54b1-810f-4f07-82b9-76a5067f5743"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You will find all the files mentioned in this chapter in <em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="81ab77aa-87a1-4d5a-9b0f-614c267845d6">Web_App_Platform_&lt;version&gt;_linux_no_install.zip</ac:inline-comment-marker></em> or on the software download website (except for Teamwork Cloud keystore and SSL certificate):</p><ul><li>The <em>&lt;webapp-charts_root&gt;/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/webapps</em> directory contains application .war files.</li><li>The <em>&lt;webapp-charts_root&gt;/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf</em> directory contains other configuration files.</li><li>The <em>&lt;webapp-charts_root&gt;/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/conf</em> directory contains <em>server.xml</em>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol></li><li>Use the same SSL certificate and key that were used for the Teamwork Cloud server. You can copy the key<span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> and </span>certificate<span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> files from the Teamwork Cloud server.</span></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">If you use only the .p12 file on the Teamwork Cloud server, execute the following command to extract<em> </em>the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="6159b8d6-58e0-4022-9707-9269754557e2">certificate</ac:inline-comment-marker> and key<span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </span><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">from the .p12 file:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0f2f1a3d-ef05-43b8-acf4-a977c5521adf"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[openssl pkcs12 -in INFILE.p12 -out twcloud.crt -nokeys
openssl pkcs12 -in INFILE.p12 -out twcloud.key -nodes –nocerts]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the following command to create a directory for each application you are going to use:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ebba471c-8983-46a8-bd2e-672d7345d0fc"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[mkdir -p imagebuild/{admin-console,authentication,artemis,collaborator,document-exporter,oslc,reports,resources,resource-usage-map,simulation,webapp}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="29246c9f-0fbe-431f-a936-0d1b2c8a6510">Copy all files listed below to their directories (except the <em>artemis</em> directory). You will find the Docker files for each service in <em><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="dockerfiles.zip" /></ac:link>.</em> After placing the Docker files in their appropriate directories, rename them to <em>Dockerfile</em> </ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="29246c9f-0fbe-431f-a936-0d1b2c8a6510">as shown below:</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>admin-console</th></tr><tr><td><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><em>admin.war</em></li><li><em>Dockerfile</em></li></ul></li></ul></td></tr><tr><th>authentication</th></tr><tr><td><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><em>authentication.war</em></li><li><em>Dockerfile</em></li></ul></li></ul></td></tr><tr><th colspan="1">collaborator</th></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><em>collaborator.war</em></li><li><em>Dockerfile</em></li><li><em>data</em> (The directory is located in <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/</em>. Before copying, remove the <em>document-exporter</em> directory from the <em>data</em> directory.)</li></ul></li></ul></td></tr><tr><th colspan="1">document-exporter</th></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><em>Dockerfile</em></li><li><em>data</em> (The directory is located in <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/</em>. Before copying, remove the <em>collaborator</em> directory from the <em>data</em> directory.)</li><li><em>document-exporter.war</em></li><li><em>collaborator.war</em></li></ul></li></ul></td></tr><tr><th colspan="1">oslc</th></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><em>collaborator.war</em></li><li><em>Dockerfile</em></li></ul></li></ul></td></tr><tr><th colspan="1">reports</th></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><em>Dockerfile</em></li><li><em>reports.war</em></li></ul></li></ul></td></tr><tr><th colspan="1">resource-usage-map</th></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><em>Dockerfile</em></li><li><em>resource-usage-map.war</em></li></ul></li></ul></td></tr><tr><th colspan="1">resources</th></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><em>Dockerfile</em></li><li><em>resources.war</em></li></ul></li></ul></td></tr><tr><th colspan="1">simulation</th></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><em>Dockerfile</em></li><li><em>simulation.war</em></li></ul></li></ul></td></tr><tr><th colspan="1">webapp</th></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;"><ul><li><em>Dockerfile</em></li><li><em>webapp.war</em></li></ul></li></ul></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the following command to start the local Docker registry:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4492c765-1243-4370-b779-cb877286f978"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker run -d -p 5000:5000 --restart=always --name registry registry:2]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5fb7ad51-fe04-4e3b-b785-f54effb9b75e"><ac:rich-text-body>In this case, a local Docker registry is run on the K8S cluster admin host. It uses port 5000, so make sure this port is open on your host firewall and accessible to your Kubernetes cluster.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e77d086f-90e9-4982-a467-c09761c2f549"><ac:rich-text-body>This local Docker registry should be used for testing purposes only and is not considered production-ready. In the production environment, run the image registry with TLS and authentication.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Add your repo URL (FQDN of the K8S cluster admin host) to the Docker </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">/etc/docker/daemon.json</em> file.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">If the <em>daemon.json</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> file does not exist, create it. Assuming there are no other settings in the file, it should have the following content:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7dc2674a-6227-49ca-bc4d-6fa51a349241"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[{
"insecure-registries": ["admin.example.com:5000"]
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the following command to restart the Docker service:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d8c5b931-ceae-4ec1-91fc-85797b73e539"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[sudo systemctl restart docker]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">If the registry container has started, proceed with building the Artemis image (for more information, refer to <a href="https://github.com/apache/activemq-artemis/tree/main/artemis-docker">https://github.com/apache/activemq-artemis/tree/main/artemis-docker</a>):</p><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the following command:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b9a1a11-e872-4bb1-9820-8e0667ce1bff"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[cd imagebuild
git clone https://github.com/apache/activemq-artemis.git
cd activemq-artemis/artemis-docker]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the following command to download <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="54d20aa2-85b2-44e9-8dc5-b24331aa2f08">Artemis binari</ac:inline-comment-marker>es:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="32afa569-4153-4418-a02d-43c637e803de"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[wget https://archive.apache.org/dist/activemq/activemq-artemis/2.28.0/apache-artemis-2.28.0-bin.zip]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Extract the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="e125f62a-0544-4e45-8eaf-fad2ee6c8f63">following</ac:inline-comment-marker> file:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="295b5d80-8a68-41cb-9d50-d4b2343513e6"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[unzip apache-artemis-2.28.0-bin.zip]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the following commands to build the image:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="27d35c41-be46-49bd-882f-266255883546"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[./prepare-docker.sh --from-local-dist --local-dist-path ./apache-artemis-2.20.0
cd apache-artemis-2.20.0
docker build -f ./docker/Dockerfile-centos7-11 -t artemis-centos .]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the following command to tag the built image:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="caec09f9-8f48-42b4-9503-85b4d5c2a96a"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker tag artemis-centos admin.example.com:5000/artemis:2.20]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the following command to push the image to your repository:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="25f8ffef-e12e-4d92-8251-62c51188b805"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker push admin.example.com:5000/artemis:2.20]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a216496b-e6eb-4da3-ac06-dab8c7acc8fd">Execute the commands</ac:inline-comment-marker> below for each service that you want to build images for inside their own directories, as shown in the examples in steps 15a, 15b, and 15c.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="602bd7af-d60b-419d-899a-8aada180f38a"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker build -f Dockerfile -t {APP_NAME} .
docker tag {APP_NAME}:{VERSION} {IMAGE_REPO_URL}:5000/{APP_NAME}:{VERSION}
docker push {IMAGE_REPO_URL}/{APP_NAME}:{VERSION}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6f0d92e3-39cb-42e8-8eda-3fdc849c7245"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>{IMAGE_REPO_URL} - your image repository URL. It can differ depending on the image registry provider.</li><li>{APP_NAME} - application name.</li><li>{VERSION} - version to be used for the tag.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">The following command example builds images for Admin Console:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5486b58e-af03-467b-a822-6481750e1e68"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[cd imagebuild/admin-console
docker build -f Dockerfile -t admin .]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">The following command example tags the built image:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5ab14d59-e17f-4ed4-85fc-201d08cee491"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker tag admin: latest admin.example.com:5000/admin:latest]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">The following command example pushes the image to the registry:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="438ae99d-8fd5-4728-a293-11d1c47a36cc"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[docker push admin.example.com:5000/admin:latest]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol></li><li>To be able to pull images to your Kubernetes cluster nodes, add your image repository to the Containerd configuration on <strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="54565691-0126-4cd2-a7bb-30f26e289e3d">all cluster nodes</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="54565691-0126-4cd2-a7bb-30f26e289e3d">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Edit the <em>/etc/containerd/config.toml</em> file by adding the following lines to the &quot;mirrors&quot; part:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ce222a88-8173-4138-a552-f7b758ba4225"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[[plugins."io.containerd.grpc.v1.cri".registry.mirrors."admin.exmple.com:5000"]
              endpoint = ["http://admin.example.com:5000"]

      [plugins."io.containerd.grpc.v1.cri".registry.configs." admin.example.com"]
        [plugins."io.containerd.grpc.v1.cri".registry.configs." admin.example.com".tls]
         insecure_skip_verify = true]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c63a4c74-d89c-4c9d-aac0-6498bdbdfcf9"><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="5ae96b19-0293-49ab-85a2-1074b9b201a8">Keep in mind that alignment is very important, as shown in the example below.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="5ae96b19-0293-49ab-85a2-1074b9b201a8"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="containerd.PNG" /></ac:image></ac:inline-comment-marker></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the following command to restart Containerd services on all cluster nodes:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d6cf23e9-9d85-47bd-b5cb-28e3f773cd06"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[sudo systemctl restart containerd]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="89022803-cbec-4545-a4d4-69e01172ba40">Execute the following commands to add dependency repos:</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="52de2975-7421-4c79-a834-cc9f1e4983a8"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[helm repo add kedacore https://kedacore.github.io/charts
helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx
helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
helm repo update

cd ${webapp-charts_directory}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Execute the following command to update chart dependencies:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8df50fea-2bd9-4e51-aa0d-235d00b476ff"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[helm dependency update
]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the command below to install Custom Resource Definitions (CRD). This will create all services and <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="f7817c29-0707-48e1-b73a-84d4978dcffd">deployments.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a68aac8d-fc23-4c69-b620-52384e06e5c3"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[helm install keda kedacore/keda --namespace keda --create-namespace --wait --version 2.6.0]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5455dd26-4fac-4cf9-b582-8a4fffe4da2f"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>If you use a later Kubernetes version than v1.26, change the keda version in the command to 2.9.0</li><li><p>You can check the status of the services and pods with this command: kubectl get all</p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="40193a47-68ba-47d4-b310-2501fa049398"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Keda and MetalLB should be added as separate resources.</p><p>Since CRDs (being a globally shared resource) are fragile, you have to assume that it is shared across multiple namespaces and groups of users once a CRD is installed. For that reason, installing, modifying, and deleting CRDs is a process that has ramifications for all users and systems of that cluster.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute the following command to deploy MetalLB:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ad547b73-491b-4661-b40f-c9c14c588563"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[helm install metallb bitnami/metallb --namespace metallb-system --create-namespace --wait]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">To check the MetalLB deployment status, execute the following command on the control plane node or the K8S cluster admin host:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0d47f1b6-fd45-4096-b82c-b5e6f001f240"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[kubectl get -n metallb-system all]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="1b0486f7-18a2-464a-8dd9-c5ff59e3d2ef">You should see an output similar to this:</ac:inline-comment-marker><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="output_example.png" /></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="038c7120-7519-4732-b67a-6b9c18d2126f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you see that the MetalLB status is 'Running,' but READY is 0/1, give it some time to start and repeat the command.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="3e54ccb9-d1a8-438e-b77f-b8c3d1f6e84f">Create configuration files for MetalLB:<br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cb6eacae-5b3f-4a1d-9226-a96773e97bc8"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Make sure to complete all the substeps of this step to create and apply both configurations. Otherwise, the product will not work properly.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="3e54ccb9-d1a8-438e-b77f-b8c3d1f6e84f"> </ac:inline-comment-marker></p><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Go the the directory where you extracted the <em>webapp-charts.zip</em> file:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="210b7c98-e7fe-4e92-b8e2-2173961529bb"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[cd <webapp-charts>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </span></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Edit the </span><em>metalLB/metallb_ipadresspool.yaml</em> file as shown below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a1e65202-5d7c-40f3-b00a-53180ac65e38"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[apiVersion: metallb.io/v1beta1
kind: IPAddressPool
metadata:
  name: first-pool
  namespace: metallb-system
spec:
  addresses:
  - 192.168.1.1/32
  #- 192.168.10.0/24
  #- 192.168.9.1-192.168.9.5
  #- fc00:f853:0ccd:e799::/124]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d613f3a4-258b-45c1-9c7c-4bfedd33b66a"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Your IT department should give you a reserved IP address or a range of IP addresses (depending on your needs) with a DNS A record that points to this IP address.</span></li><li>Configuration files should be formatted in <em>.yaml</em>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Edit the crd/<em>metalLB/l2advertisement.yaml</em> file as shown below. You can change the metadata name as needed. Keep in mind that the ipAddressPool name should match the specifications.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="71524bf8-eed4-4074-a4e0-8f79fd8c3ede"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[apiVersion: metallb.io/v1beta1
kind: L2Advertisement
metadata:
  name: example
  namespace: metallb-system
spec:
  ipAddressPools:
  - first-poolkubectl describe l2advertisements]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Execute the following command to apply the MetalLB configuration:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6b952c1c-3ea6-476d-af7f-b97518abd2ee"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[kubectl apply -f metalLB/metallb_ipaddresspool.yaml]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Execute the following command to check the applied configuration:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c3161007-1679-4c04-817c-94c75001ab15"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[kubectl describe ipaddresspools.metallb.io first-pool -n metallb-system]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Execute the following command to create the MetalLB advertisement:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f58546e0-a1a0-4f17-9c5c-502f9ee82c95"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[kubectl apply -f metalLB/l2advertisement.yaml]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Execute the following command to check the advertisement configuration:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2e35e320-3fd7-4441-9159-2c8a10b23c80"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[kubectl describe l2advertisements.metallb.io example -n metallb-system]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="da86080c-b04a-436a-b9fe-9bb72e6691fa">Execute the commands below to collect configuration files for all applications and copy them to their directories. These files will be used to create config maps and mount them into pods.<br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0fa4dbf-4d25-488c-9b9c-8980bc5f8d1d"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Make sure that you work in the <span>directory where you extracted the <em>webapp-charts.zip</em> file (&lt;</span>webapp-charts_root&gt; directory).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="81638fa3-bec9-4383-a387-ff639ff69929"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[mkdir -p configs/{auth,cassandra,ssl,tomcat,twcloud,wap}
cp <webapp-charts_root>/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/authserver.properties configs/auth
cp <webapp-charts_root>/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/authserver.properties configs/logback.xml revision.txt webappplatform.properties configs/wap
cp <webapp-charts_root>/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/webappplatform.properties configs/wap
cp <webapp-charts_root>/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/ logback.xml configs/wap
cp <webapp-charts_root>/CATIANoMagicServices/WebAppPlatform/shared/conf/revision.txt configs/wap
cp <webapp-charts_root>/CATIANoMagicServices/conf/catalina.properties server.xml configs/tomcat
<webapp-charts_root>/keystore.p12 configs/twcloud
cp <webapp-charts_root>/teamworkcloud.crt configs/twcloud]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="da86080c-b04a-436a-b9fe-9bb72e6691fa"> </ac:inline-comment-marker></p></li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="da86080c-b04a-436a-b9fe-9bb72e6691fa">In the <em>webapp-charts root</em> directory, find the v</ac:inline-comment-marker><em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="da86080c-b04a-436a-b9fe-9bb72e6691fa">alues.yaml</ac:inline-comment-marker></em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="da86080c-b04a-436a-b9fe-9bb72e6691fa"> file and provide the values of the parameters inside the file to enable or disable specific applications or parts of the configuration.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Do one of the following:</p><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Execute this command in the <em>webapp-charts root</em> directory to deploy services to the default namespace:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="35c8cbe0-109b-4c86-a65b-8721e0b4e764"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[helm install webapp .]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Execute this command in the helm parent chart directory to create a namespace and deploy services in this namespace:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6cd7b34e-ff79-4767-826a-7ece62a9f7e6"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[helm install webapp . --namespace=wap --create-namespace --wait]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="de256884-47cb-4ca6-9294-ef6b56ea5e45"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This helm chart includes Zookeeper and Ingress-nginx as dependencies. They will be deployed automatically.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">After all web applications are deployed, execute the following command to check their <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="e345ba45-bd1d-49e3-bea6-3bbca50538b4">status:</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1ba43432-4b8c-455f-8f68-b1d81f038dde"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[kubectl get all]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5c6792b1-83f5-4892-bb7e-c37c75f84abf"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>All pods should be READY:1/1, STATUS:Running.</li><li>The ingress-nginx service should be Type:LoadBalancer.</li><li>EXTERNAL-IP should match the address that you provided in the MetalLB configuration.</li></ul><p>If pods do not run, check for problems by executing this command:</p><pre>kubectl describe pod pod_name</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Execute the following command to check Ingress rules:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="37a4d037-5379-4d05-bfd3-78d0deb73c47"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[kubectl describe ingress]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">You should get an output similar to this:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="07c5cbd4-0fa8-4b89-b05a-446de8bc859e"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Name:             ingress.resource
Namespace:        default
Address:          **.**.***.***
Default backend:  default-http-backend:80 (<error: endpoints "default-http-backend" not found>)
TLS:
  webapp-tls-secret terminates nm-wap10639.dsone.3ds.com
Rules:
  Host                       Path  Backends
  ----                       ----  --------
  nm-wap10639.dsone.3ds.com
                             /admin                webapp-adminconsole:8443 (10.233.105.39:8443)
                             /authentication       webapp-authentication:8443 (10.233.88.69:8443)
                             /collaborator         webapp-collaborator:8443 (10.233.88.75:8443)
                             /document-exporter    webapp-docexporter:8443 (10.233.105.22:8443)
                             /oslc                 webapp-oslc:8443 (10.233.105.23:8443)
                             /reports              webapp-reports:8443 (10.233.73.246:8443)
                             /resources            webapp-resources:8443 (10.233.88.67:8443)
                             /resource-usage-map   webapp-rum:8443 (10.233.105.40:8443)
                             /simulation           webapp-simulation:8443 (10.233.88.123:8443)
                             /webapp               webapp-webapp:8443 (10.233.105.29:8443)
Annotations:                 meta.helm.sh/release-name: webapp
                             meta.helm.sh/release-namespace: default
                             nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/affinity: cookie
                             nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/affinity-canary-behavior: sticky
                             nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/affinity-mode: persistent
                             nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/backend-protocol: https
                             nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size: 100m
                             nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-connect-timeout: 600
                             nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-read-timeout: 600
                             nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-send-timeout: 600
                             nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/send-timeout: 600
                             nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/session-cookie-name: COOKIE
Events:                      <none>

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </span></p></li><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Test the product deployment:</span><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In an internet browser, go to <em>domain_name/webapp</em> (the DNS A record that points to the Ingress external IP).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e86a164e-e4a0-4dd2-aee3-dde0200f624a"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you added a DNS record to bind the domain name to the IP address, you can use the domain name instead of the IP. The browser should show a warning because of the self-signed certificate. Accept it to proceed, and you will be redirected to the Teamwork Cloud Authentication web page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="web_app_platform_welcome_screen.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></p></li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="95b2b7dc-2bd1-4cdc-a959-2da58782efb0">Log in with your credentials, and you will be redirected back to </ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="5ee328c2-1625-47cc-82d4-c0d30ec6010e"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="95b2b7dc-2bd1-4cdc-a959-2da58782efb0">Web Application Platfor</ac:inline-comment-marker>m</ac:inline-comment-marker>.</li></ol></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137991479 space=MCS2024x version=4 -->
## PAGE 00077: Disabling authentication with username and password

- page_id: `137991479`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137991479/Disabling+authentication+with+username+and+password
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-12-11T14:40:19.352+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Deployment Guide > Configuration > Authentication server
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can disable authentication with a username and password as described below.

This feature affects only the logins using the Authentication server, such as logging into Magic Collaboration Studio from web applications and from a modeling tool using SSO.

To disable authentication with a username and password

1. Go to the <install_root>\WebAppPlatform\shared\conf directory and open the authserver.properties file.
2. In the file, find the authentication.disable.credentials property and change its value to true .
3. Restart the webapp service .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can disable authentication with a username and password as described below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b37c49da-7404-4f6c-94e9-cdf9b95a6809"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This feature affects only the logins using the Authentication server, such as logging into Magic Collaboration Studio from web applications and from a modeling tool using SSO.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To disable authentication with a username and password</p><hr /><ol><li>Go to the <em>&lt;install_root&gt;\WebAppPlatform\shared\conf</em> directory and open the <strong>authserver.properties</strong> file.</li><li>In the file, find the <strong>authentication.disable.credentials</strong> property and change its value to <em>true</em>.</li><li>Restart the webapp <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="aa7e1d15-ab00-42b2-9f8c-6cb5292258bc">service</ac:inline-comment-marker>.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137992147 space=MCS2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00078: Disabling or removing a LDAP server

- page_id: `137992147`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137992147/Disabling+or+removing+a+LDAP+server
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-29T10:51:11.188+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > User Guide > Admin web applications > Settings application > Managing LDAP servers
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can disable a connection to LDAP server temporarily or remove it from Magic Collaboration Studio system. External users from the disabled LDAP server can request for authentication from other connected LDAP servers. If the result is valid, they will be able to log into Teamwork Cloud Admin. Those who are in the system when you disable the connection to the LDAP server can still access and perform all operations as normal, however as soon as they log out from the Teamwork Cloud Admin, their status will change to **Disable**. You still can import users from the LDAP server even though it has been disabled.

To disable an LDAP directory

1. On the **LDAP management** page, select a LDAP server you want to disable, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''].
2. From the list choose to Disable . A confirmation dialog will appear.
3. Click Disable .

An LDAP directory icon that is disabled turn grey in the **LDAP management** page. You can enable this server by clicking [IMAGE alt='' src=''] and selecting the **Enable.**

To remove an LDAP directory

1. On the LDAP management page, select an LDAP directory you want to remove, click [ATTACHMENT filename='menu.png'] .
2. Click Remove . A confirmation dialog will appear.
3. Click Remove .

If user removes LDAP configuration, he will not be able to synchronize imported users and groups associated with removed LDAP.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can disable a connection to LDAP server temporarily or remove it from Magic Collaboration Studio system. External users from the disabled LDAP server can request for authentication from other connected LDAP servers. If the result is valid, they will be able to log into Teamwork Cloud Admin. Those who are in the system when you disable the connection to the LDAP server can still access and perform all operations as normal, however as soon as they log out from the Teamwork Cloud Admin, their status will change to <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Disable</strong>. You still can import users from the LDAP server even though it has been disabled. </p><p><br /></p><p>To disable an LDAP directory</p><hr /><ol><li><p>On the <strong>LDAP management</strong> page, select a LDAP server you want to disable, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS2024x" ri:content-title="Disabling or removing a LDAP server" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</p></li><li>From the list choose to <strong>Disable</strong>. A confirmation dialog will appear.</li><li>Click <strong>Disable</strong>. </li></ol><p>An LDAP directory icon that is disabled turn grey in the  <strong>LDAP management</strong> page. You can enable this server by clicking <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS2024x" ri:content-title="Using the app bar" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> and selecting the <strong>Enable.</strong> </p><p><br /></p><p>To remove an LDAP directory </p><hr /><ol><li>On the <strong>LDAP management</strong> page, select an LDAP directory you want to remove, click<ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS2024x" ri:content-title="Disabling or removing a LDAP server" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</li><li>Click <strong>Remove</strong>. A confirmation dialog will appear.</li><li>Click <strong>Remove</strong>. </li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e930de2f-871d-4195-b5c4-d79db982faf4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If user removes LDAP configuration, he will not be able to synchronize imported users and groups associated with removed LDAP.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````
