<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137992294 space=MCS2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00100: Filtering usages

- page_id: `137992294`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137992294/Filtering+usages
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-29T10:51:21.476+02:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > User Guide > Resource Usage Map
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

After [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating resource usage maps' space='MCS2024x'], you can use the Filters pane located on the left side of the application portal to choose what types of usages should be displayed in the map. As you can see below, there two filter groups: main filters which you can use in every resource usage map, and filters for suspicious usages which only exist if there are suspicious usages in a map.

To filter resources in a resource usage map, select the check-boxes next to the types of usages you want to view and clear the check-boxes next to the types of usages you want to hide.

#### TIP: Why a map is empty?

Why a map is empty?

If no usages in your resource usage map meet the criteria of the selected filters, the map is displayed as empty. Select more filters to see resource usages in the map.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### This example of a resource usage map illustrates different types of filters you can use in the Filters pane.

The following tables describes all the filters you can use in a resource usage map:

| Resource usage map filter | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Main filters | Main filters are located at the top of the Filters pane. You can use them in every resource usage map to show or hide non-suspicious usages and standard profiles including local used projects. |
| Display non-suspicious usages | Select this filter to show non-suspicious usages in a resource usage map. |
| Display standard profiles | Select this filter to show the usages of standard profiles and local used projects in a resource usage map. |
| Filters for suspicious usages | Filters for suspicious usages are located under the main filters in the Filters pane. You can use them to show or hide different types of suspicious usages. However these filters are displayed only if there are suspicious usages in a resource usage map. |
| Inconsistent version usages | Select this filter to show the resources that use different versions of the same resource directly and through other used projects. |
| Cyclic usages | Select this filter to show the resources that use themselves through other used projects and form a usage loop. |
| Unused resources | Select this filter to show the resources that are not used by other resources stored in the Magic Collaboration Studio repository. The Unused resources filter can only be displayed in a map created for all the resources in the repository. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>After <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS2024x" ri:content-title="Creating resource usage maps" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[creating a resource usage map]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, you can use the Filters pane located on the left side of the application portal to choose what types of usages should be displayed in the map. As you can see below, there two filter groups: main filters which you can use in every resource usage map, and filters for suspicious usages which only exist if there are suspicious usages in a map.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To filter resources in a resource usage map, select the check-boxes next to the types of usages you want to view and clear the check-boxes next to the types of usages you want to hide.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3df5ff02-5fc0-4496-9a1e-81651afaec23"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Why a map is empty?</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If no usages in your resource usage map meet the criteria of the selected filters, the map is displayed as empty. Select more filters to see resource usages in the map.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="filters_pane.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MCS2024x" ri:content-title="Filtering usages" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">This example of a resource usage map illustrates different types of filters you can use in the Filters pane.</h6><p>The following tables describes all the filters you can use in a resource usage map:</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 76.4125%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 20.2586%;" /><col style="width: 79.7414%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Resource usage map filter</th><th>Description</th></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Main filters</strong></td><td>Main filters are located at the top of the Filters pane. You can use them in every resource usage map to show or hide non-suspicious usages and standard profiles including local used projects.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Display non-suspicious usages</strong></td><td>Select this filter to show non-suspicious usages in a resource usage map.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;"><strong>Display standard profiles</strong></td><td>Select this filter to show the usages of standard profiles and local used projects in a resource usage map.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;" colspan="1"><strong>Filters for suspicious usages</strong></td><td colspan="1"><span>Filters for suspicious usages are located under the main filters in the Filters pane. <span>You can use them to show or hide different types of suspicious usages. </span>However these filters are displayed only if there are suspicious usages in a resource usage map.</span></td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;" colspan="1"><strong>Inconsistent version usages</strong></td><td colspan="1">Select this filter to show the resources that use different versions of the same resource directly and through other used projects.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;" colspan="1"><strong>Cyclic usages</strong></td><td colspan="1">Select this filter to show the resources that use themselves through other used projects and form a usage loop.</td></tr><tr><td style="text-align: left;" colspan="1"><strong>Unused resources</strong></td><td colspan="1">Select this filter to show the resources that are not used by other resources stored in the Magic Collaboration Studio repository. The <strong>Unused resources</strong> filter can only be displayed in a map created for all the resources in the repository.</td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137992680 space=MCS2024x version=4 -->
## PAGE 00101: General convention

- page_id: `137992680`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137992680/General+convention
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2026-01-12T13:55:36.674+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Developer Guide > REST APIs
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Unless specified otherwise, this document assumes that the following specific operations call HTTP methods.

| Operation | HTTP method | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Get or read | GET | Get data. |
| Create | POST | Create new data. |
| Replace attributes | PUT | Replace all existing attributes with the new ones. Attributes with unspecified new values will be reset to null values. |
| Update a (sub)set of attributes | PATCH | Replace some attributes only with the new ones. Unspecified attributes remain unchanged. |
| Delete | DELETE | Delete data. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Unless specified otherwise, this document assumes that the following specific operations call HTTP methods.</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 1739.75px;"><colgroup><col style="width: 350.156px;" /><col style="width: 214.609px;" /><col style="width: 1173.98px;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th style="background-color: rgb(241,242,244);">Operation</th><th style="background-color: rgb(241,242,244);">HTTP method</th><th style="background-color: rgb(241,242,244);">Meaning</th></tr><tr><td>Get or read</td><td>GET</td><td>Get data.</td></tr><tr><td>Create</td><td>POST</td><td>Create new data.</td></tr><tr><td>Replace attributes</td><td>PUT</td><td>Replace all existing attributes with the new ones. Attributes with unspecified new values will be reset to null values.</td></tr><tr><td>Update a (sub)set of attributes</td><td>PATCH</td><td>Replace some attributes only with the new ones. Unspecified attributes remain unchanged.</td></tr><tr><td>Delete</td><td>DELETE</td><td>Delete data.</td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137991481 space=MCS2024x version=3 -->
## PAGE 00102: General parameters

- page_id: `137991481`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137991481/General+parameters
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2026-01-20T08:20:49.878+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Deployment Guide > Configuration > Authentication server
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following parameters are used with the authentication server to ensure proper working functionality.

| Parameter | Description | Default value |
| --- | --- | --- |
| authentication.token.expirity | The authentication ID token expiration time in seconds. | 900 |
| authentication.code.token.expirity | The authentication code expiration time in seconds. | 15 |
| authentication.implicit.token.expirity | The authentication ID token expiration time in seconds for the implicit authentication flow (e.g., used for MagicDraw UML). | 86400 |
| authentication.unlimited.token.expirity | Authentication ID token that has extremely long expiration time and should only be used in server-server requests when there is a need to perform regular batch jobs. | 630720000 |
| authentication.client.ids | The valid client IDs, separated by commas. | MAGICDRAW,webApplicationPlatform,twcSynchronizationManager |
| authentication.client.implicit | The implicit authentication flow client IDs (separated by commas). | MAGICDRAW |
| authentication.client.unlimited | Client IDs that use token ID of unlimited expiration (separated by commas). | twcSynchronizationManager |
| authentication.redirect.uri.whitelist | A list of allowed redirect URIs. The authentication server client should pass a valid redirect URI to an authorized endpoint. | https://${webapp.server.ip}:8443/,https://${twc.server.ip}:8111/,https://md_redirect/ |
| authentication.disable.credentials | Ability to disable username/password authentication if external user authentication (CAC, SAML) is used. | false |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

style="width: 17.208%;" /><col style="width: 61.5779%;" /><col style="width: 21.214%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Description</th><th colspan="1">Default value</th></tr><tr><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-yellow-bolder,#533f04);">authentication.token.expirity</span></td><td>The authentication ID token expiration time in seconds.</td><td colspan="1"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="17efc81d-2a86-4ecd-a27b-4ff8d34bd359"><span>900</span></ac:inline-comment-marker></td></tr><tr><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-yellow-bolder,#533f04);">authentication.code.token.expirity</span></td><td>The authentication code expiration time in seconds.</td><td colspan="1">15</td></tr><tr><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-yellow-bolder,#533f04);">authentication.implicit.token.expirity</span></td><td>The authentication ID token expiration time in seconds for the implicit authentication flow (e.g., used for MagicDraw UML).</td><td colspan="1">86400</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">authentication.unlimited.token.expirity</td><td colspan="1">Authentication ID token that has extremely long expiration time and should only be used in server-server requests when there is a need to perform regular batch jobs.</td><td colspan="1">630720000</td></tr><tr><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-yellow-bolder,#533f04);">authentication.client.ids</span></td><td>The valid client IDs, separated by commas.</td><td colspan="1"><p>MAGICDRAW,webApplicationPlatform,twcSynchronizationManager</p></td></tr><tr><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-yellow-bolder,#533f04);">authentication.client.implicit</span></td><td>The implicit authentication flow client IDs (separated by commas).</td><td colspan="1">MAGICDRAW</td></tr><tr><td colspan="1">authentication.client.unlimited</td><td colspan="1">Client IDs that use token ID of unlimited expiration (separated by commas).</td><td colspan="1">twcSynchronizationManager</td></tr><tr><td><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-yellow-bolder,#533f04);">authentication.redirect.uri.whitelist</span></td><td>A list of allowed redirect URIs. The authentication server client should pass a valid redirect URI to an authorized endpoint.</td><td colspan="1"><p>https://${webapp.server.ip}:8443/,</p><p>https://${twc.server.ip}:8111/,</p><p><span class="nolink">https://md_redirect/</span></p></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><p>authentication.disable.credentials</p></td><td colspan="1">Ability to disable username/password authentication if external user authentication (CAC, SAML) is used.</td><td colspan="1">false</td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137992314 space=MCS2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00103: Generating a report

- page_id: `137992314`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137992314/Generating+a+report
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T11:04:22.775+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > User Guide > Reports application
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To generate a report

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Accessing the Reports application' space='']Open the **Reports** application.
2. Select the desired report template. In this example we use the User details report template: [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Select the scope of users. You can select a single, multiple, or all users.
4. Click **Next**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Next, select the format of the report and choose whether to include assigned roles in the reported data: [ATTACHMENT filename='report_options.png']
6. Click Finish and Save to generate and save your report. Below is an example of a User details report file: [ATTACHMENT filename='user_details_report.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">To generate a report</span></p><p><br /></p><hr /><ol><li style="text-align: left;"><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Accessing the Reports application" /><ac:link-body>Open the <strong>Reports</strong> application</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li style="text-align: left;"><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">Select the desired report template. In this example we use the User details report template:</span><br /><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);"><br /></span><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Reports_application_User_details.png" /></ac:image><br /></span><br /><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);"><br /></span></li><li style="text-align: left;"><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">Select the scope of users. You can select a single, multiple, or all users.</span></li><li style="text-align: left;"><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">Click <strong>Next</strong>.</span><br /><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);"><br /></span><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="User_details_report_scope.png" /></ac:image><br /></span><br /><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);"><br /></span></li><li style="text-align: left;"><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);">Next, select the format of the report and choose whether to include assigned roles in the reported data:</span><br /><span style="color: rgb(14,16,26);"><br /></span><ac:image ac:height="250"><ri:attachment ri:filename="report_options.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li style="text-align: left;">Click <strong>Finish</strong> and <strong>Save</strong> to generate and save your report. Below is an example of a User details report file:<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="user_details_report.png" /></ac:image></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137992274 space=MCS2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00105: Generating resource revisions report

- page_id: `137992274`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137992274/Generating+resource+revisions+report
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T11:00:50.039+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > User Guide > Resources application
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can export the history of any Magic Collaboration Studio project into an Excel spreadsheet. Sharing project revision reports can be a quick and easy way to show progress to team leads or project managers.

To generate and download the resource revisions report

1. Navigate to the Resources application and go to the category where the relevant resource is located.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Click [ATTACHMENT filename='menu.png'] next to the resource and select Generate revisions report.
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Viewing and editing resource details' space='']Open the **Resource** pane. On the resource details card click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] and select Generate revisions report .
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Viewing and editing resource details' space='']Open the **Resource** pane . On the top right of the History card click the icon displayed below: [ATTACHMENT filename='generate_revisions_report_icon_on_history_card.png']
3. In the window that opens, select Download to generate and download the report.

Below is an example of a resource revisions report in XSLS format:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Resource revisions report.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can export the history of any Magic Collaboration Studio project into an Excel spreadsheet. Sharing project revision reports can be a quick and easy way to show progress to team leads or project managers.</p><p>To generate and download the resource revisions report</p><hr /><ol><li>Navigate to the <strong>Resources</strong> application and <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">go to the category where the relevant resource is located.</span></li><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Do one of the following:</span></span><ul><li>Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png" /></ac:image> next to the resource and select<strong> Generate revisions report.</strong></li><li><span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Viewing and editing resource details" /><ac:link-body>Open the <strong>Resource</strong> pane</ac:link-body></ac:link>. On the resource details card click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png" /></ac:image> and </span>select<strong> Generate revisions report</strong><span>.</span></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Viewing and editing resource details" /><ac:link-body>Open the <strong>Resource</strong> pane</ac:link-body></ac:link>. On the top right of the <strong>History</strong> card click the icon displayed below:<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="generate_revisions_report_icon_on_history_card.png" /></ac:image></li></ul></li><li>In the window that opens, select<strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> Download </strong>to generate and download the report.</li></ol><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Below is an example of a resource revisions report in XSLS format:</span></p><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Resource revisions report" ac:alt="Resource revisions report"><ri:attachment ri:filename="resource_revisions_report.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Resource revisions report.</span></h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137992452 space=MCS2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00106: Getting document information

- page_id: `137992452`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137992452/Getting+document+information
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-12-11T15:25:53.377+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > User Guide > Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud > Structure of the document portal
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Each Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document contains detailed document and project information. The following steps outline how to access this information.

Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document information

1. Open a Cameo Collaborator document.
2. In the app bar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='menu.png'] and select Document information .

The document information screen opens, displaying the following information:

- Document information:
  - Document creation time
  - The name of the user who created the document
  - The last time the document was published
  - The name of the user who published the latest document version
  - The number of the latest document version
  - Indication if commenting and/or editing is enabled and the template that was used to publish the document
- Project information:
  - Project creation time
  - The name of the user who created the project
  - The last time the project was modified
  - The name of the last user to modify the project
  - The number of the latest project version
  - Project branch name

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The example of the Cameo Collaborator document information screen.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Each Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document contains detailed document and project information. The following steps outline how to access this information.</p><p><br /></p><p>Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document information</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a Cameo Collaborator document.</li><li>In the app bar, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png" /></ac:image> and select <strong>Document information</strong>.</li></ol><p><br />The document information screen opens, displaying the following information:</p><ul><li>Document information:<ul><li>Document creation time</li><li>The name of the user who created the document</li><li>The last time the document was published</li><li>The name of the user who published the latest document version</li><li>The number of the latest document version</li><li>Indication if commenting and/or editing is enabled and the template that was used to publish the document</li></ul></li><li>Project information:<ul><li>Project creation time</li><li>The name of the user who created the project</li><li>The last time the project was modified</li><li>The name of the last user to modify the project</li><li>The number of the latest project version</li><li>Project branch name</li></ul></li></ul><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="document_information.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The example of the Cameo Collaborator document information screen.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137992233 space=MCS2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00107: Getting resource links

- page_id: `137992233`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137992233/Getting+resource+links
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T11:07:43.353+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > User Guide > Resources application > Working with resources
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you want to share a Magic Collaboration Studio project or Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document link, you can get it as described below.

To get a Magic Collaboration Studio project link

1. Open the Resources application and go to the category where the project is located.
2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='menu.png'] next to the project, and select Get project link .
3. When the window with the project link opens, click Copy .

Once you complete the steps above, the link to the project is copied to your clipboard.

To get a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document link

1. Open the Resources application and go to the category where the project is located.
2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='menu.png'] next to the project, and do one of the following:
  - Select Get resource link to if you want to get the link for registered users.
  - Select Get public resource link if you want to share the link publicly.
3. When the window with the project link opens, click Copy .

Once you complete the steps above, the document link is copied to your clipboard and you can share it with reviewers.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>If you want to share a Magic Collaboration Studio project or Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document link, you can get it as described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To get a Magic Collaboration Studio project link</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Resources application and go to the category where the project is located.</li><li>Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png" /></ac:image> next to the project, and select <strong>Get project link</strong>.</li><li>When the window with the project link opens, click <strong>Copy</strong>.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>Once you complete the steps above, the link to the project is copied to your clipboard.</p><p><br /></p><p>To get a Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud document link</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Resources application and go to the category where the project is located.</li><li>Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="menu.png" /></ac:image> next to the project, and do one of the following:<ul><li>Select <strong>Get resource link</strong> to if you want to get the link for registered users.</li><li>Select <strong>Get public resource link</strong> if you want to share the link publicly.</li></ul></li><li>When the window with the project link opens, click <strong>Copy</strong>.</li></ol><p><br />Once you complete the steps above, the document link is copied to your clipboard and you can share it with reviewers.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137991575 space=MCS2024x version=4 -->
## PAGE 00108: Global element usage indexing and search

- page_id: `137991575`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137991575/Global+element+usage+indexing+and+search
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-12-11T14:44:12.148+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Administration Guide > Magic Collaboration Studio administration
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

**5**

You can index resources for their element usages within the selected scope of the Magic Collaboration Studio repository and later query these usages through the modeling tool. This enables you to evaluate what impact modifying or deleting certain model elements will have on other models that are using it. To enable this functionality, Magic Collaboration Studio needs to be incorporated with a dedicated search engine - Elasticsearch. This component makes it possible to index model element usage data and serve it quickly when queried from a modeling tool.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Conceptual solution for the global element usage indexing and search functionality.

##### Elasticsearch system requirements and installation

To start using the global element usage search functionality, you need to install Elasticsearch (v7 series).

###### System requirements

- We recommend using NVMe SSD disks for the Elastic search instance.
- The -Xmx value should be set to 4GB as a minimum.
- The expected indexing speed under recommended hardware settings is roughly 1 revision per second.
- The following formula can be used to roughly determine the required disk space per single Indexing Configuration:

*Used Project size (element count) x 400 bytes x N x # of Using Projects (specified scope)*,

where N equals the number of times both using and used elements get changed throughout their history (N tends to range from 4 to 10 as noted from experiments with production data). 
The formula also assumes that actual element-level reference ratio is 15-20% from all of the elements in a Used Project. 
E.g. A DB size of 250GBs yields an index size of 60GBs under the above mentioned conditions.

###### Installing Elasticsearch

Go to [https://www.elastic.co/downloads/elasticsearch](https://www.elastic.co/downloads/elasticsearch) and install Elasticsearch (v7 series).

We highly recommend deploying Elasticsearch on a machine separate from Magic Collaboration Studio and Cassandra due to additional server resource consumption.

In the Elasticsearch installation directory, open the **jvm.options** file and make sure the **-Xms** and **-Xmx** properties are uncommented.

##### Enabling global element usage indexing and search

Once you install Elasticsearch, configure Magic Collaboration Studio as described below to start using the global element usage search functionality.

To enable global element usage search

1. Go to <install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration , open the application.conf file, and add the following lines at the end of the file:
  1. To enable the querying component, add the following property and set it to *true*:
  2. To enable resource indexing, add the following property and set it to *true*:
  3. If Elasticsearch and Magic Collaboration Studio run on different machines, add the following line: "]]>
  4. If Elasticsearch is set up to listen on a different port from the default one (9200), add the following line: ]]>
2. Restart Magic Collaboration Studio services.

Now you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating indexing configurations' space=''] in the Settings application and use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Global element usage search' space='MD2024x'] in a modeling tool.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f2f9305e-8e16-4e99-a1bb-be6ea4467ec2"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">5</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>You can index resources for their element usages within the selected scope of the Magic Collaboration Studio repository and later query these usages through the modeling tool. This enables you to evaluate what impact modifying or deleting certain model elements will have on other models that are using it. To enable this functionality, Magic Collaboration Studio needs to be incorporated with a <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b0a6d07a-4c4f-4aa3-a312-1e3879f531a0">dedicated search engine</ac:inline-comment-marker> - Elasticsearch. This component makes it possible to index model element usage data and serve it quickly when queried from a modeling tool.</p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="elastic_search_engine.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="letter-spacing: normal;line-height: 1.66667;text-align: center;color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b5ccf3f9-bb04-4b4e-95c8-9e94306c039f">Conceptual solution for the global element usage indexing and search functionality.</ac:inline-comment-marker></h6><h3 style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Elasticsearch system requirements and installation</h3><p>To start using the global element usage search functionality, you need to install Elasticsearch (v7 series).</p><h4 style="line-height: 1.42857;color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">System requirements</h4><ul><li>We recommend using NVMe SSD disks for the Elastic search instance.</li><li>The <strong>-Xmx</strong> value should be set to 4GB as a minimum.</li><li>The expected indexing speed under recommended hardware settings is roughly 1 revision per second.</li><li>The following formula can be used to roughly determine the required disk space per single Indexing Configuration:</li></ul><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;"><em>Used Project size (element count) x 400 bytes x N x # of Using Projects (specified scope)</em>,</p><p style="margin-left: 40.0px;">where N equals the number of times both using and used elements get changed throughout their history (N tends to range from 4 to 10 as noted from experiments with production data).<br />The formula also assumes that actual element-level reference ratio is 15-20% from all of the elements in a Used Project.<br />E.g. A DB size of 250GBs yields an index size of 60GBs under the above mentioned conditions.</p><h4 style="line-height: 1.42857;color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Installing Elasticsearch</h4><p class="auto-cursor-target" style="margin-top: 0.0px;">Go to <a style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-blue-bolder,#0c66e4);" href="https://www.elastic.co/downloads/elasticsearch">https://www.elastic.co/downloads/elasticsearch</a> and <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="e5d559f6-7017-4534-ada1-39d5fbe4a6a6">install</ac:inline-comment-marker> Elasticsearch (v7 series).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0b774c91-43d3-4482-bdf6-9fa4b2d825cf"><ac:rich-text-body><p>We highly recommend deploying Elasticsearch on a machine separate from Magic Collaboration Studio and Cassandra due to additional server resource consumption.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b944e1f5-7953-4add-ae0a-068df46c880c"><ac:rich-text-body><p>In the Elasticsearch installation directory, open the <strong>jvm.options</strong> file and make sure the <strong>-Xms</strong> and <strong>-Xmx</strong> properties are uncommented.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Enabling global element usage indexing and search</h3><p>Once you install Elasticsearch, configure Magic Collaboration Studio as described below to start using the global element usage search functionality.</p><p>To enable global element usage search</p><hr /><ol><li>Go to <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/configuration</em>, open the <strong>application.conf</strong> file, and add the following lines at the end of the file:<ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">To enable the querying component, add the following property and set it to <em>true</em>:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b5b1e630-fa85-4e30-a0e0-426c83a1dcd8"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.server.actor.query.component-enabled=true]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">To enable resource indexing, add the following property and set it to <em>true</em>:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="81f02ab8-6e99-4a00-b904-65f77c3a8786"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.indexer.enabled = true]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">If Elasticsearch and Magic Collaboration Studio run on different machines, add the following line:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="52486740-8419-4838-9a3d-59d681c99c28"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.query.es.node.host = "<es.host.or.ip>"]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">If Elasticsearch is set up to listen on a different port from the default one (9200), add the following line:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4c056d48-30ce-47af-8dfb-a37e7b682a4f"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.query.es.node.port = <es.port>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li><li>Restart Magic Collaboration Studio services.</li></ol><p><br />Now you can <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating indexing configurations" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[index resources for their element usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in the Settings application and use the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Global element usage search" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[global element usage search functionality]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in a modeling tool.</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137991456 space=MCS2024x version=15 -->
## PAGE 00109: Hardening Cassandra

- page_id: `137991456`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137991456/Hardening+Cassandra
- version_number: 15
- version_date: `2025-12-11T13:46:52.599+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Deployment Guide > Configuration > Hardening Magic Collaboration Studio
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

**Used scripts**

**[ATTACHMENT filename='harden_cassandra_ports.sh']**

##### Cassandra Authentication

By default, Cassandra is deployed with the AllowAllAuthenticator. This authenticator, as the name implies, is an anonymous authenticator which performs no checks.

If you require authenticated connections, you will need to make changes to your **cassandra.yaml** and change the authenticator to PasswordAuthenticator. If you are running a multi-node Cassandra cluster, you need to change the replication factor of the system_auth keyspace.

Detailed instructions can be found at [https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html](https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html)

After making the changes to Cassandra, you will need to update the Teamwork Cloud configurations to utilize authentication.

The required changes are as follows:

```text

```

Where *newcassandrauser* and *newcassandrapassword* correspond to the user and credentials which you configured in Cassandra.

###### SSL authentication for Cassandra

To enable SSL authentication for Cassandra

1. Set the following properties in the**authserver.properties**file:
2. If the Cassandra certificate is**not signed by a public certificate** authority, import Cassandra public certificate to respectiveTeamwork Cloud ANDAuthserver (WebAppPlatform)locationsby running the following command: bashDJango **Java cacerts default locations:**
  - for Teamwork Cloud (example): /etc/alternatives/jre_17/lib/security/cacerts
  - for Authserver (WebAppPlatform): *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/jre/lib/security/cacerts*

##### Cassandra Encrypted Connections

Starting from Teamwork Cloud Version 2021x and onwards, encrypted communication with Cassandra is supported. To achieve this, the [configuration of secure SSL communication](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/TWCloud2024x/Advanced+Teamwork+Cloud+configuration#AdvancedTeamworkCloudconfiguration-ConfiguringsecureSSLcommunicationbetweenTeamworkCloudandCassandra) is needed.

##### Data Encryption at Rest

Open source Cassandra does not support encryption at rest. If you require encryption at rest, you need to use a disk encryption layer.

##### Configuration file property encryption

Apache Cassandra (open source version) does not support encrypting cassandra.yaml properties values.

##### Port access for firewall

When installing on Linux using our deployment scripts, all of the ports required by Cassandra for inter-node communication, as well as for the Teamwork Cloud nodes to communicate with Cassandra nodes are opened globally. This configuration is deployed mostly to facilitate testing of the environment upon installation, prior to taking any measures to harden the installation. If we check the firewall upon installation, we will see an output similar to the one below:

```text

```

In our deployment, we create a firewall service definition to facilitate the management of the rules. This file is located in */etc/firewalld/services/cassandra.xml*, and contains the following:

```text
cassandra
    cassandra
    
    
        
        
        


]]>
```

The first step in securing Cassandra is to limit traffic only to Cassandra and Teamwork Cloud Nodes. In the example below, we have a single node Cassandra/Teamwork Cloud installation, with its IP address set to 10.254.254.56.

As can be seen below, the firewall configuration has been modified to only allow access from itself.

```text

```

The process to follow is to remove the general firewall allowance to the Cassandra service. After that, we want to ensure that if direct port rule assignments were made, they are removed. Finally, we want to create a set of rich rules which will allow access only to the required nodes.

If your deployment consists of a single-node or multi-node cluster where both Teamwork Cloud and Cassandra reside on the same nodes, automate this process by downloading and using the *[ATTACHMENT filename='harden_cassandra_ports.sh']* script. The script is structured in such a way that it will output the rich rule commands to the screen to facilitate copying and modifying to add nodes, which is needed if you have Teamwork Cloud nodes that are not part of the Cassandra cluster.

The *[ATTACHMENT filename='harden_cassandra_ports.sh']* script should be executed on all nodes of a multi-node Cassandra cluster, with all nodes being in an operational state.

For Windows users: Create firewall rules restricting access on the aforementioned ports only to authorized nodes - Cassandra nodes and Teamwork Cloud Nodes.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="441f6e6d-d138-490c-9299-ddc35fbb26a3"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Used scripts</strong></p><p><strong><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="harden_cassandra_ports.sh" /></ac:link></strong></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3><span>Cassandra Authentication</span></h3><p>By default, Cassandra is deployed with the AllowAllAuthenticator. This authenticator, as the name implies, is an anonymous authenticator which performs no checks.</p><p>If you require authenticated connections, you will need to make changes to your <strong>cassandra.yaml</strong> and change the authenticator to PasswordAuthenticator. If you are running a multi-node Cassandra cluster, you need to change the replication factor of the system_auth keyspace.</p><p>Detailed instructions can be found at <a href="https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html">https://docs.datastax.com/en/ddacsecurity/doc/ddacsecurity/secureConfigNativeAuth.html</a></p><p>After making the changes to Cassandra, you will need to update the Teamwork Cloud configurations to utilize authentication.</p><p>The required changes are as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6bda044e-e33a-4315-a378-87de036a7130"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">application.conf</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[auth-provider {
     # Enable this section to enable the cassandra authentication.
     class = PlainTextAuthProvider
     username = newcassandrauser
     password = newcassandrapassword
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Where <em>newcassandrauser</em> and <em>newcassandrapassword</em> correspond to the user and credentials which you configured in Cassandra.</p><h4>SSL authentication for Cassandra</h4><p>To enable SSL authentication for Cassandra</p><hr /><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span>Set the following properties in the</span><strong> authserver.properties </strong><span>file:<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="00dbe8d4-6e87-462e-b8c4-b4366b6f1192"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[cassandra.ssl.enabled=true
cassandra.ssl.ignore.certificate=false]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span> </span></p></li><li><p style="text-align: left;">If the Cassandra certificate is<span> </span><strong>not signed by a public certificate</strong> authority, i<span>mport Cassandra public certificate to respective </span><span>Teamwork Cloud AND</span><span> Authserver (WebAppPlatform)</span><span> locations </span><span>by running the following command:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9fea3b67-aa63-4e76-a75a-1878c4bb8957"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">bash</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="theme">DJango</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[keytool.exe -import -trustcacerts -keystore cacerts -storepass changeit -noprompt -alias 'server-alias' -file server.cer]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>Java cacerts default locations:</strong></p><ul><li><span>for Teamwork Cloud (example): </span><em>/etc/alternatives/jre_17/lib/security/cacerts</em></li><li><p style="text-align: left;">for Authserver (WebAppPlatform): <em>&lt;install_root&gt;<span>/WebAppPlatform/jre/lib/security/cacerts</span></em></p></li></ul></li></ol><h3>Cassandra Encrypted Connections</h3><p>Starting from Teamwork Cloud Version 2021x and onwards, encrypted communication with Cassandra is supported. To achieve this, the <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/TWCloud2024x/Advanced+Teamwork+Cloud+configuration#AdvancedTeamworkCloudconfiguration-ConfiguringsecureSSLcommunicationbetweenTeamworkCloudandCassandra">configuration of secure SSL communication</a> is needed.</p><h3>Data Encryption at Rest</h3><p>Open source Cassandra does not support encryption at rest. If you require encryption at rest, you need to use a disk encryption layer.</p><h3>Configuration file property encryption</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Apache Cassandra (open source version) does not support encrypting cassandra.yaml properties values.</span></p><h3>Port access for firewall</h3><p>When installing on Linux using our deployment scripts, all of the ports required by Cassandra for inter-node communication, as well as for the Teamwork Cloud nodes to communicate with Cassandra nodes are opened globally. This configuration is deployed mostly to facilitate testing of the environment upon installation, prior to taking any measures to harden the installation. If we check the firewall upon installation, we will see an output similar to the one below:</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bd77a12c-ab8a-4d19-913f-8c564b224951"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# firewall-cmd --list-all
public (active)
  target: default
  icmp-block-inversion: no
  interfaces: eth0
  sources:
  services: cassandra lmadmin ssh twcloud
  ports:
  protocols:
  masquerade: no
  forward-ports:
  source-ports:
  icmp-blocks:
  rich rules:


]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In our deployment, we create a firewall service definition to facilitate the management of the rules. This file is located in <em>/etc/firewalld/services/cassandra.xml</em>, and contains the following:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5d4e3771-a365-4d1b-9eff-877db6449eae"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# cat /etc/firewalld/services/cassandra.xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<service version="1.0">
    <short>cassandra</short>
    <description>cassandra</description>
    <port port="7000" protocol="tcp"/>
    <port port="7001" protocol="tcp"/>
        <port port="9042" protocol="tcp"/>
        <port port="9160" protocol="tcp"/>
        <port port="9142" protocol="tcp"/>
</service>

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The first step in securing Cassandra is to limit traffic only to Cassandra and Teamwork Cloud Nodes. In the example below, we have a single node Cassandra/Teamwork Cloud installation, with its IP address set to 10.254.254.56.</p><p>As can be seen below, the firewall configuration has been modified to only allow access from itself.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f056107c-d95f-4d93-870b-dc3a8af27d77"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# firewall-cmd --list-all
public (active)
  target: default
  icmp-block-inversion: no
  interfaces: eth0
  sources:
  services: lmadmin ssh twcloud
  ports:
  protocols:
  masquerade: no
  forward-ports:
  source-ports:
  icmp-blocks:
  rich rules:
        rule family="ipv4" source address="10.254.254.56" service name="cassandra" accept

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The process to follow is to remove the general firewall allowance to the Cassandra service. After that, we want to ensure that if direct port rule assignments were made, they are removed.  Finally, we want to create a set of rich rules which will allow access only to the required nodes.</p><p>If your deployment consists of a single-node or multi-node cluster where both Teamwork Cloud and Cassandra reside on the same nodes, automate this process by downloading and using the <em><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="harden_cassandra_ports.sh"><ri:page ri:space-key="TWCloudTWRT" ri:content-title="Hardening Cassandra" /></ri:attachment></ac:link></em> script. The script is structured in such a way that it will output the rich rule commands to the screen to facilitate copying and modifying to add nodes, which is needed if you have Teamwork Cloud nodes that are not part of the Cassandra cluster.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8b5c56b3-de21-478c-9c44-2dc920964c70"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <em><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="harden_cassandra_ports.sh"><ri:page ri:space-key="TWCloudTWRT" ri:content-title="Hardening Cassandra" /></ri:attachment></ac:link></em> script should be executed on all nodes of a multi-node Cassandra cluster, with all nodes being in an operational state.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="327ecc3e-fa51-4949-b474-aef3d8c51f32"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For Windows users: Create firewall rules restricting access on the aforementioned ports only to authorized nodes - Cassandra nodes and Teamwork Cloud Nodes.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137991451 space=MCS2024x version=5 -->
## PAGE 00110: Hardening Magic Collaboration Studio

- page_id: `137991451`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137991451/Hardening+Magic+Collaboration+Studio
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-12-11T13:46:44.986+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Deployment Guide > Configuration
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The default shipping configuration of Magic Collaboration Studio is not a hardened configuration. When hardening an installation, some variables can render the installation inoperative, such as incompatibility of the supported ciphers in a certificate and the supported ciphers in the hardened configuration. Furthermore, the default configurations assume that the deployment is behind a secure infrastructure, and therefore required ports are globally allowed.

Since some of Magic Collaboration Studio's infrastructure relies on available components, newly discovered vulnerabilities need to be mitigated during the life-cycle of the installation.

The sections below cover potentially exploitable vulnerabilities of the different components, as well as various steps to mitigate them depending on the policies of the deploying organization.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The default shipping configuration of Magic Collaboration Studio is not a hardened configuration. When hardening an installation, some variables can render the installation inoperative, such as incompatibility of the supported ciphers in a certificate and the supported ciphers in the hardened configuration. Furthermore, the default configurations assume that the deployment is behind a secure infrastructure, and therefore required ports are globally allowed.</p><p>Since some of Magic Collaboration Studio's infrastructure relies on available components, newly discovered vulnerabilities need to be mitigated during the life-cycle of the installation.</p><p>The sections below cover potentially exploitable vulnerabilities of the different components, as well as various steps to mitigate them depending on the policies of the deploying organization.</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="193c8f84-d2b5-4a92-89c1-8bb933275011" /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137991458 space=MCS2024x version=8 -->
## PAGE 00111: Hardening Teamwork Cloud

- page_id: `137991458`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137991458/Hardening+Teamwork+Cloud
- version_number: 8
- version_date: `2025-12-11T13:47:00.510+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Deployment Guide > Configuration > Hardening Magic Collaboration Studio
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

**Used files**

- [ATTACHMENT filename='twc.java.security']

##### Protocols and Ciphers

Teamwork Cloud consists of 2 Java-based services - Teamwork Cloud (twcloud) and WebApp (webapp).

Teamwork Cloud requires Java 17 (its location varies depending on how it was deployed), whereas Web Application Platform uses a bundled Java 17, located in*<install_root>/WebAppPlatform**/jre/*.

Therefore, in order to harden these services, we must begin by hardening the JVM. The default settings for the JVM are located in **java.security**.

#### EXPAND: How to Check Protocols and Ciphers for a Specific Port

How to Check Protocols and Ciphers for a Specific Port

We can check the ciphers/protocols being used by the applications using nmap (version 7.x) or TestSSLServer.jar, available from [https://community.rsa.com/docs/DOC-45511](https://community.rsa.com/docs/DOC-45511).

As an example, below is a scan using both tools against a default installation. In this example, we will be testing port 8111, the Teamwork Cloud port.

```text

```

As can be observed above, the default configuration using OpenJDK 1.8.0_242 is allowing TLS v1.0 and v1.1, which are deprecated. Additionally, we can see that several key exchanges are taking place using dh1024.

We then proceed to harden the configuration.

Since we are dealing with ciphers, you need to make sure that you do not disable a cipher required by your certificates.

After hardening the VM, we end up with a different set of allowed ciphers and protocols, as shown below.

```text

```

The process of hardening the JVM requires making some changes to the **java.security** file. While these can be made directly, the downside is that if you upgrade your JVM, you will have to reapply your changes.

However, we can place our modifications in our own file, and simply pass a parameter to the JVM upon invocation so that it will apply our changes.

For example, we can create a file */home/twcloud/twc.java.security*, and pass a parameter to the JVM in the form of *-Djava.security.properties=/home/twcloud/twc.java.security.*

Our hardened security settings are as shown below:

```text

```

To apply these settings, we need to make changes to both the Teamwork Cloud and Web App Platform services.

1. **Teamwork Cloud service** 
For Linux, edit *<install_root>/TeamworkCloud/jvm.options* and add a line as shown below: jvm.options For Windows, edit the registry key *Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\TeamworkCloud\Parameters\Java\Options* and append the setting pointing to your security overrides to the bottom of the settings.
2. **Web App Platform service** 
For Linux, *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/bin/setenv.sh*and add the directive to the**JVM_OPTS**variable as shown below: setenv.sh On Windows, you need to edit the registry key *Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\WebApp\Parameters\Java\Options* and append the setting pointing to your security overrides to the bottom of the settings.

JMX

By default, the Teamwork Cloud service activates a JMX remote port to facilitate application monitoring. The default configuration does not contain any form of authentication.

On Linux, the configuration is located in *<install_root>/jvm.options*.

On Windows, it is located in registry key *Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\TeamworkCloud\Parameters\Java\Options*.

```text

```

These settings can be removed, thereby removing JMX remote access.

If you would like to allow remote JMX access but require authentication, you can do so by adding settings. For complete documentation, please refer to the Java documentation.

As an example, the below configuration adds password authentication:

```text

```

As can be seen, we are pointing to a set of files (*/home/twcloud/jmx.password* and */home/twcloud/jmx.access*) that control who can access these files.

The vulnerability vector is one whereby JMX could be exploited to execute code. To prevent this, we allow only an authenticated user (jmx.password) who has read-only rights (jmx.access).

```text

```

```text

```

In this example, we created a user (monitoring) with a password (DqzbksT4ET), who can only read values via Remote JMX, but cannot write or execute anything via JMX.

The password and access files have a very stringent ownership requirement. They need to be owned by the user running the process and be accessible exclusively to that user.

For example, in our default installation, the Teamwork Cloud user is running the Teamwork Cloud service. Therefore, the files need to be owned by Teamwork Cloud and have full rights (rwx) by Teamwork Cloud, and only Teamwork Cloud.

# ll jmx.* 
-rwx------. 1 twcloud twcloud 20 Mar 16 15:11 jmx.access 
-rwx------. 1 twcloud twcloud 26 Apr 21 10:41 jmx.password

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d4856707-70e9-495d-aab7-e7299dc459dd"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Used files</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="twc.java.security" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Protocols and Ciphers</h3><p style="text-align: left;">Teamwork Cloud consists of 2 Java-based services - Teamwork Cloud (twcloud) and WebApp (webapp). </p><p style="text-align: left;">Teamwork Cloud requires Java 17 (its location varies depending on how it was deployed), whereas Web Application Platform uses a bundled Java 17, located in<span> <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform</em></span><em>/jre/</em>. </p><p>Therefore, in order to harden these services, we must begin by hardening the JVM. The default settings for the JVM are located in <strong>java.security</strong>. </p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="expand" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="58d87dcd-e8d2-4242-a939-315d9bf88e79"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">How to Check Protocols and Ciphers for a Specific Port</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>We can check the ciphers/protocols being used by the applications using nmap (version 7.x) or TestSSLServer.jar, available from <a href="https://community.rsa.com/docs/DOC-45511">https://community.rsa.com/docs/DOC-45511</a>.</p><p>As an example, below is a scan using both tools against a default installation. In this example, we will be testing port 8111, the Teamwork Cloud port.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="798c7a79-116d-4564-a5ed-dd4df3dbe396"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 8111 127.0.0.1
Starting Nmap 7.80 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2020-04-21 17:32 MDT
Nmap scan report for localhost (127.0.0.1)
Host is up (0.00014s latency).

PORT     STATE SERVICE
8111/tcp open  unknown
| ssl-enum-ciphers:
|   TLSv1.0:
|     ciphers:
|       TLS_DHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (dh 1024) - A
|       TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (secp256r1) - A
|       TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (rsa 2048) - A
|     compressors:
|       NULL
|     cipher preference: client
|     warnings:
|       Key exchange (dh 1024) of lower strength than certificate key
|   TLSv1.1:
|     ciphers:
|       TLS_DHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (dh 1024) - A
|       TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (secp256r1) - A
|       TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (rsa 2048) - A
|     compressors:
|       NULL
|     cipher preference: client
|     warnings:
|       Key exchange (dh 1024) of lower strength than certificate key
|   TLSv1.2:
|     ciphers:
|       TLS_DHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (dh 1024) - A
|       TLS_DHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256 (dh 1024) - A
|       TLS_DHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 (dh 1024) - A
|       TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (secp256r1) - A
|       TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256 (secp256r1) - A
|       TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 (secp256r1) - A
|       TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (rsa 2048) - A
|       TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256 (rsa 2048) - A
|       TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 (rsa 2048) - A
|     compressors:
|       NULL
|     cipher preference: client
|     warnings:
|       Key exchange (dh 1024) of lower strength than certificate key
|_  least strength: A

Nmap done: 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 0.76 seconds

# java -jar TestSSLServer.jar 127.0.0.1 8111
Supported versions: TLSv1.0 TLSv1.1 TLSv1.2
Deflate compression: no
Supported cipher suites (ORDER IS NOT SIGNIFICANT):
  TLSv1.0
     RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
     DHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
     TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
  (TLSv1.1: idem)
  TLSv1.2
     RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
     DHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
     RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256
     DHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256
     TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
     TLS_DHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
     TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
     TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256
     TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
----------------------
Server certificate(s):
  a241e1c3b957bd14e6e0242fd012f75853eef243: CN=X.X.X.X
----------------------
Minimal encryption strength:     strong encryption (96-bit or more)
Achievable encryption strength:  strong encryption (96-bit or more)
BEAST status: vulnerable
CRIME status: protected
]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>As can be observed above, the default configuration using OpenJDK 1.8.0_242 is allowing TLS v1.0 and v1.1, which are deprecated. Additionally, we can see that several key exchanges are taking place using dh1024.</p><p>We then proceed to harden the configuration.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6d26db2b-3402-4764-af4a-f5818c033087"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Since we are dealing with ciphers, you need to make sure that you do not disable a cipher required by your certificates.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">After hardening the VM, we end up with a different set of allowed ciphers and protocols, as shown below.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b69b38da-6232-426c-a48f-0ff72449201f"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[# nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 8111 127.0.0.1
Starting Nmap 7.70 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2020-04-21 17:44 MDT
Nmap scan report for localhost (127.0.0.1)
Host is up (0.00015s latency).

PORT     STATE SERVICE
8111/tcp open  unknown
| ssl-enum-ciphers:
|   TLSv1.2:
|     ciphers:
|       TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256 (secp256r1) - A
|       TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 (secp256r1) - A
|       TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256 (rsa 2048) - A
|       TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 (rsa 2048) - A
|     compressors:
|       NULL
|     cipher preference: client
|_  least strength: A

Nmap done: 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 0.95 seconds

# java -jar TestSSLServer.jar 127.0.0.1 8111
Supported versions: TLSv1.2
Deflate compression: no
Supported cipher suites (ORDER IS NOT SIGNIFICANT):
  TLSv1.2
     RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256
     TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
----------------------
Server certificate(s):
  71ed3969e41a94877c51aacb87d995af4a12b6d9: CN=x.x.x.x
----------------------
Minimal encryption strength:     strong encryption (96-bit or more)
Achievable encryption strength:  strong encryption (96-bit or more)
BEAST status: protected
CRIME status: protected

]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><span>The process of hardening the JVM requires making some changes to the</span> <strong> java.security</strong> <span> file. While these can be made directly, the downside is that if you upgrade your JVM, you will have to reapply your changes.</span></p><p>However, we can place our modifications in our own file, and simply pass a parameter to the JVM upon invocation so that it will apply our changes.</p><p>For example, we can create a file <em>/home/twcloud/twc.java.security</em>, and pass a parameter to the JVM in the form of <em>-Djava.security.properties=/home/twcloud/twc.java.security.</em></p><p>Our hardened security settings are as shown below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="256f442c-3904-48d3-a5d6-77d4180568b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">twc.java.security</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[jdk.tls.disabledAlgorithms=SSLv3, TLSv1, TLSv1.1, RC4, DES, MD5withRSA, DH keySize < 2048, \
    EC keySize < 224, 3DES_EDE_CBC, anon, RSA keySize < 2048, SHA1, DHE, NULL
jdk.tls.ephemeralDHKeySize=2048
jdk.tls.rejectClientInitiatedRenegotiation=true]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To apply these settings, we need to make changes to both the Teamwork Cloud and Web App Platform services.</p><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Teamwork Cloud service</strong><br />For Linux, edit <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/jvm.options</em> and add a line as shown below:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95a5eec9-e510-4b2e-83ba-58305a4e045d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">jvm.options</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[.
.
-Dorg.jboss.netty.epollBugWorkaround=true
-Dio.netty.epollBugWorkaround=true
-Djava.security.properties=/home/twcloud/twc.java.security]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">For Windows, edit the registry key <em>Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\TeamworkCloud\Parameters\Java\Options</em> and append the setting pointing to your security overrides to the bottom of the settings.</p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Web App Platform service</strong><br />For Linux, <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/WebAppPlatform/bin/setenv.sh</em><span> and add the directive to the </span><strong>JVM_OPTS</strong><span> variable as shown below:</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fd75d0d2-dc1e-47c0-abe3-47315042c495"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">setenv.sh</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[JVM_OPTS="-server -XX:+UseParallelGC  -Xms4096M -Xmx8192M -Djava.security.properties=/home/twcloud/twc.java.security"]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">On Windows, you need to edit the registry key <em>Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\WebApp\Parameters\Java\Options</em> and append the setting pointing to your security overrides to the bottom of the settings.</p></li></ol><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span>JMX</span></p><p>By default, the Teamwork Cloud service activates a JMX remote port to facilitate application monitoring. The default configuration does not contain any form of authentication.</p><p>On Linux, the configuration is located in <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/jvm.options</em>.</p><p>On Windows, it is located in registry key <em>Computer\HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\Apache Software Foundation\Procrun 2.0\TeamworkCloud\Parameters\Java\Options</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ee0e6979-fec2-409c-a91f-f875354235f4"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.port=2468
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.rmi.port=2468
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.local.only=false
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.authenticate=false
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.ssl=false]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>These settings can be removed, thereby removing JMX remote access.</p><p>If you would like to allow remote JMX access but require authentication, you can do so by adding settings. For complete documentation, please refer to the Java documentation.</p><p>As an example, the below configuration adds password authentication:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bfa4d566-8587-4912-a860-07c8023eefdb"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.port=2468
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.rmi.port=2468
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.local.only=false
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.authenticate=true
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.password.file=/home/twcloud/jmx.password
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.access.file=/home/twcloud/jmx.access
-Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote.ssl=false]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>As can be seen, we are pointing to a set of files (<em>/home/twcloud/jmx.password</em> and <em>/home/twcloud/jmx.access</em>) that control who can access these files.</p><p>The vulnerability vector is one whereby JMX could be exploited to execute code. To prevent this, we allow only an authenticated user (jmx.password) who has read-only rights (jmx.access).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f86ad40c-fd02-431b-a566-612bef7211f5"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">jmx.password</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[monitoring  DqzbksT4ET]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="71cedc20-ff24-4541-84f1-cba195e11ec8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">jmx.access</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[monitoring readonly]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In this example, we created a user (monitoring) with a password (DqzbksT4ET), who can only read values via Remote JMX, but cannot write or execute anything via JMX.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="38d23f67-a185-4e56-8663-0774e38f386c"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The password and access files have a very stringent ownership requirement. They need to be owned by the user running the process and be accessible exclusively to that user.</p><p>For example, in our default installation, the Teamwork Cloud user is running the Teamwork Cloud service. Therefore, the files need to be owned by Teamwork Cloud and have full rights (rwx) by Teamwork Cloud, and only Teamwork Cloud.</p><p># ll jmx.*<br />-rwx------. 1 twcloud twcloud 20 Mar 16 15:11 jmx.access<br />-rwx------. 1 twcloud twcloud 26 Apr 21 10:41 jmx.password</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=137991460 space=MCS2024x version=8 -->
## PAGE 00112: Hardening Web Application Platform

- page_id: `137991460`
- space_key: `MCS2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MCS2024x/pages/137991460/Hardening+Web+Application+Platform
- version_number: 8
- version_date: `2026-03-09T12:41:37.371+01:00`
- ancestors: Magic Collaboration Studio Documentation > Deployment Guide > Configuration > Hardening Magic Collaboration Studio
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

**Used scripts**

- [ATTACHMENT filename='upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='upgrade_jdk_webapp.sh']

The Web Application Platform, as deployed by our installer, runs on a bundled Apache Tomcat. As such, best practices for hardening Apache Tomcat should be followed.

Although we have already constrained ciphers and protocols at the JVM level, it is best practice to do so at the Tomcat configuration level. We also need to address issues such as secure cookies, disable XSS on foreign sites, and also remove default directories published as part of the default installation. The official Tomcat documentation covers a large portion of this (recommended reading) - [Tomcat Security Considerations](https://tomcat.apache.org/tomcat-10.1-doc/security-howto.html). Additionally, there are a plethora of documents online covering all aspects of securing Tomcat to OWASP standards.

##### server.xml

Follow steps below to make changes to *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml.*

1. Disable the AJP connector unless you specifically intend to use it by doing the following: 
Replace the following lines: ]]> with the lines below: <Connector port="8009" protocol="AJP/1.3" redirectPort="8443" />]]>
2. Prevent the Tomcat instance from advertising what server is being used when an error is encountered by doing the hollowing:Add the following lines at the end of the file, right above the closing </Host> tag. ]]>

- The shutdown port 8005 is disabled by default. If you need to enable it, we recommend to change the default SHUTDOWN password. To enable the port 8005, open the <install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml file and do the following: Replace the following line: ]]> with the line below: ]]>

- The port 8080 with redirection is disabled by default. To enable it open the <install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/server.xml file and uncomment the following lines: -->]]>

##### web.xml

Having completed the configuration of server.xml, we will now proceed to configure *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/conf/web.xml.*

We need to ensure that cookies are constrained to HTTPS.

```text
30
		
		true
		true
		
    ]]>
```

You only need to insert the following lines:

**<cookie-config>**

**<http-only>true</http-only>**

**<secure>true</secure>**

**</cookie-config>**

##### Tomcat installation

Having made the necessary changes to the configuration files, we will now proceed to remove all of the default applications in the tomcat distribution, which could expose our installation to external vulnerabilities.

If we look at a directory of *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/webapps*, we will see the following:

```text

```

As you can see, in addition to *webapp.war* and the *webapp* directory, there are additional directories, containing applications, which could potentially be exploited.

You want to remove *docs*, *examples*, *host-manager*, *manager*, and *ROOT*.

When you remove the ROOT application directory, accessing [https://ip_address:8443](https://ip_address:8443) will no longer display the Apache Tomcat default landing page.

##### Upgrading and patching Apache Tomcat

Web Application Platform uses Apache Tomcat as the Java web application server for deployment. Upgrading or patching is often required to keep your Tomcat server up to date with all the latest security and performance improvements.

Refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''] page for version compatibility between Web Application Platform and Apache Tomcat releases.

The files required for upgrading and patching Tomcat are a compilation of JAR files residing in*<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/bin* and *<install_root>/WebAppPlatform/lib*.

Always make a backup of these directories before performing any upgrade or patch.

For Linux server deployment, use the*upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh*script to automatically update your instance to the target Tomcat version (set in script). This script can perform installation either from a locally-stored package or pull from Apache’s server (internet access required).

To upgrade Apache Tomcat on Linux using script

1. Download *upgrade_tomcat_webapp.sh* script.
2. For a server with internet access:
  1. Execute the script and specify the desired Tomcat version when prompted.

**For offline installation:**

1. 
  1. Download the Apache Tomcat package from https://archive.apache.org/dist/tomcat (tar.gz and zip files are both supported.)
  2. Execute the script and confirm installation from a local package.

To manually "slip-stream" an update without having to fully replace the Tomcat installation, replace the existing JAR files in these directories with ones from the newer Apache Tomcat package. After replacing the JAR files, make sure the new files’ permission and ownership are same as before.

##### Upgrading Web Application Platform bundled JRE

To automatically upgrade the JRE, download and run the *upgrade_jdk_webapp.sh* script. When prompted, provide the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Third-Party Component Compatibility' space=''].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
